_doc: Court-vs-Knesset case library: real, source-verified Israeli Supreme Court cases that struck or HOLLOWED OUT Knesset legislation. Compiled for public/research use. Every entry has a confirmed docket + authoritative source.

cases.name: HCJ 195/64 Southern Co. (Marbek Slaughterhouse) v. Chief Rabbinical Council (מרבק נ' המועצה הרבנית הראשית)
cases.panel: Olshan P., Agranat D.P., Landau J., Witkon J., Manny J.
cases.statute: Statutory authority of the Chief Rabbinical Council to grant kashrut certification (pre-1983 kashrut authority)
cases.doctrine: Ultra vires / narrowing construction of religious authority's powers
cases.effect_reduction: The Court held that conditions placed on kashrut licences that were unrelated to the meat's actual kosher status were ultra vires; the authority to ensure kashrut did not include power to enforce broader religious observance against the non-observant. Confined the operative reach of the certifying authority to the food itself.
cases.corrected_facts: Full official case name is "The Southern Company Ltd. and Marbek Slaughter House Ltd. v. Chief Rabbinical Council and Tel Aviv-Yaffo Religious Council" (the second respondent, the Tel Aviv-Yaffo Religious Council, is omitted in the candidate label). Exact decision date is September 27, 1964.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project (kosher-food topic page)
sources.confirms: Docket 195/64; conditions unrelated to meat's kosher status held ultra vires; authority did not include enforcing religious observance against non-observant

cases.blurb_he: סמכותה הסטטוטורית של המועצה הרבנית הראשית להעניק תעודות כשרות (לפני חוק 1983) לא בוטלה — אך בית המשפט פירש אותה בצמצום וקבע כי תנאים שאינם נוגעים לכשרות הבשר עצמו חורגים מהסמכות. כך הותיר את הטקסט על כנו אך רוקן את כוחה של הרשות הדתית לכפות שמירת מצוות רחבה על מי שאינו שומר, וכבל סמכות מנהלית לתחום המזון בלבד.
cases.blurb_en: The Chief Rabbinical Council's statutory kashrut-certification authority (pre-1983) was left textually intact — but the unelected Court read it down, holding that licence conditions unrelated to the meat's actual kosher status were ultra vires. The text survived; its real-world reach was hollowed out, stripping the religious authority of power to enforce broader observance on the non-observant and confining it to the food itself.

cases.name: Raskin v. Religious Council of Jerusalem
cases.statute: חוק איסור הונאה בכשרות, התשמ"ג-1983 — Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 1983
cases.doctrine: Purposive construction limiting administrative/religious authority to the statute's purpose (food kashrut only); ultra vires for conditions outside that purpose
cases.effect_reduction: The Court read the Kashrut Fraud Law down to its food-only purpose, holding that the authority to grant kashrut certification 'is not meant to serve as a means for imposing rules of Jewish law that do not pertain to the kosher status of the food.' This stripped the Rabbinate/religious councils of the ability they had asserted to leverage the kashrut-licensing power to police non-food religious conduct on the premises (here, conditioning certification on barring 'immodest performances'/belly dancing), narrowing the statute's de facto reach well below how the religious authorities sought to wield it.
cases.corrected_facts: Minor: the candidate year "1990" is correct as the decision date (May 27, 1990), though the docket number 465/89 reflects the 1989 filing year — both are accurate and consistent. The statute's standard English subtitle is more commonly rendered "Prohibition of Deceit" (Versa) rather than "Prohibition of Fraud," but both translate the same Hebrew "הונאה" and refer to the same Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 5743-1983 — not a substantive error.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 465/89, decided 27 May 1990; holding that kashrut-certification authority cannot impose non-food Jewish-law conditions

sources.publisher: Hebrew Wikipedia (entry on the Kashrut Fraud Law)
sources.confirms: Court demanded the Rabbinate not impose conditions unrelated to food kashrut; statute's purpose is anti-fraud about food only

cases.blurb_he: חוק איסור הונאה בכשרות, התשמ"ג-1983, נחקק כדי למנוע הטעיה לגבי כשרות מזון בלבד. בלי לפסול אות אחת מן הטקסט, פירשו השופטים את החוק בצמצום אל תכליתו הצרה וקבעו שסמכות הענקת תעודת הכשר אינה כלי לכפיית דיני יהדות שאינם נוגעים לכשרות המזון — וכך שללו מן הרבנות את כוח הרישוי שבו ביקשה להתנות הכשר בביטול מופעי בטן "לא צנועים", ורוקנו את ההישג הזה מתוכן מבלי שהמחוקק אמר על כך דבר.
cases.blurb_en: The Knesset's Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 1983 was about food kashrut alone. Without voiding a single word, the Court read it down to that narrow purpose, holding that the power to grant a kashrut certificate is not a lever for imposing rules of Jewish law unrelated to the food — stripping the Rabbinate of the licensing leverage it had used to condition certification on barring "immodest" belly-dance performances. An unelected bench hollowed the statute's real-world reach below the Rabbinate's design, on its own interpretive say-so.

cases.name: Ganimat v. State of Israel (Further Hearing) — דנ"פ 2316/95 גנימאת נ' מדינת ישראל
cases.statute: Criminal Procedure Law [Consolidated Version], 5742-1982 — detention-until-end-of-proceedings ground, section 21a(a)(1) (סעיף 21א(א)(1)). (Reported PD 49(4) 589.)
cases.doctrine: Interpretive presumption / reading pre-existing statutes 'in the spirit of' Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty. The Court held that statutes enacted before the 1992 Basic Law must be construed in conformity with it, and used that presumption to narrow the statutory detention grounds rather than to strike them.
cases.effect_reduction: The Knesset's detention provision let courts order pre-trial detention where there was 'reasonable cause to fear the accused will endanger public safety,' which prosecutors had been using by labeling whole categories of crime (e.g. car theft) a 'national scourge' (מכת מדינה) to justify near-automatic detention. The Court read the provision down: a 'national scourge' by itself does NOT establish a detention ground; the state must prove individualized, subjective danger of the specific defendant, and cannot rely on the general/categorical characterization. This gutted the routine 'category-based' detention practice the provision had enabled, shifting the burden onto the prosecution — a substantial narrowing of the statute's real-world reach without formally voiding any text.
cases.corrected_facts: Procedural posture clarification: docket is correctly דנ"פ 2316/95 (Further Hearing / דיון נוסף), reported at פ"ד מט(4) 589, decided 12 November 1995. It arose as a Further Hearing on the Court's prior decision in בש"פ 537/95, not an "appeal" in the ordinary sense; candidate's "on appeal from בש537/95" is loosely worded. Expanded seven-justice panel (Shamgar, Barak, Goldberg, Mazza, Cheshin, Strasberg-Cohen, Dorner). Statute identification (sec. 21a, Criminal Procedure Law [Consolidated Version], 5742-1982) is consistent with sources; note this is the pre-1996 detention regime (dedicated Arrests Law enacted 1996, after this decision). Core claim otherwise accurate.

sources.confirms: Docket דנ"פ 2316/95, full case name גנימאת נ' מדינת ישראל, citation פ"ד מט(4) 589, and holding on section 21a(a)(1) and burden of proof.

Dockets בש"פ 537/95 and דנ"פ 2316/95; unanimous holding that 'national scourge' alone is not a detention ground; pre-1992 statutes to be interpreted per Basic Law; burden of proving danger on the state; narrowing rather than striking.

cases.blurb_he: חוק סדר הדין הפלילי (סעיף 21א): המחוקק התיר מעצר עד תום ההליכים מחשש לפגיעה בשלום הציבור, והתביעה הסבה אותו למעצר כמעט-אוטומטי בכל "מכת מדינה". בלי לפסול אות אחת, פירש בג"ץ את ההסדר "ברוח" חוק יסוד: כבוד האדם וחירותו וקבע שמכת מדינה כשלעצמה אינה עילת מעצר — נדרשת מסוכנות אישית מוכחת. שבעה שופטים בלתי-נבחרים רוקנו הלכה למעשה את כוונת המחוקק.
cases.blurb_en: Criminal Procedure Law (sec. 21a): the Knesset let courts jail defendants pre-trial on a fear-to-public-safety ground, which prosecutors turned into near-automatic detention by branding whole crime categories a "national scourge." Without voiding a single word, the Court read the provision down "in the spirit of" Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty — holding a national scourge alone is no detention ground; the state must prove the individual defendant's danger. Seven unelected justices hollowed out the legislature's intent.

cases.name: Zandberg v. Broadcasting Authority — בג"ץ 4562/92 ח"כ אליעזר זנדברג נ' רשות השידור
cases.statute: Broadcasting Authority Law, 5725-1965; Price Stability Law (Temporary Provision), 5745-1985; and the Broadcasting Authority (Validation of Radio and Television Fees) Law, 5752-1992. (Reported PD 50(2) 793.)
cases.doctrine: The locus classicus of Israel's 'reading-down' doctrine. Barak articulated the rule that where a statute admits of multiple readings, the court should prefer the construction that keeps it constitutional, and that 'it is preferable to achieve a reduction in the scope of a statute by interpretive means rather than to achieve the same reduction by declaring part of the statute void as conflicting with a Basic Law.' Includes the principle that a reasonable interpretation is preferable to a ruling on constitutionality.
cases.effect_reduction: This is the doctrinal anchor cited across later evisceration cases (Avneri, the reasonableness-amendment dissents, etc.). On its own facts the Court construed the broadcasting/fee scheme against the Authority — holding TV fees were not exempt 'taxes' but were caught by the price freeze, invalidating unapproved 1985-1991 fee increases. Its lasting effect is the rule itself: the Court will narrow a Knesset statute's scope by interpretation 'in conformity with the Basic Laws' instead of striking it, which is the engine of interpretive evisceration. Marked as the doctrine-setting case; the practical-gutting angle on the statute itself is moderate, hence the doctrinal (not headline-strike) classification.
cases.corrected_facts:
Docket, parties (MK Eliezer Zandberg v. Broadcasting Authority et al.), year (1996), reporter citation (PD/IsrSC 50(2) 793), and statutes are all confirmed. The core doctrinal claim is ACCURATE and authoritatively sourced: the case is the anchor for "interpretive narrowing in conformity with the Basic Laws rather than striking down." Versa's ACRI v. Israel Police opinion (a provided source) quotes it with a pinpoint cite — IsrSC 50(2) 793, 812 (1996), President A. Barak: "It is better to achieve limits on a statute by interpretation rather than having to limit it by declaring part of the statute void for violating provisions of a Basic Law... A reasonable interpretation of a statute is preferable to finding it unconstitutional." The doctrine-setting / interpretive_narrowing classification is correct.

One framing correction to the "on its own facts" narrative: the candidate conflates two distinct stages. The ruling that TV fees were NOT exempt taxes and were caught by the 1985 price freeze (invalidating the 1985–1991 increases) was established in EARLIER litigation, not in 4562/92 itself. In response, the Knesset passed the Broadcasting Authority (Validation of Radio and Television Fees) Law, 5752-1992, retroactively validating the over-collected fees. HCJ 4562/92 was the challenge to THAT validation law. The Court (Barak) partially granted the petition: using interpretation plus the "concealed/latent lacuna" doctrine and a presumption of constitutional harmony, it read the validation law's retroactive reach narrowly — declining to apply it retroactively to arrears fines, on non-retroactivity-of-penalties and property-rights grounds. So the case's own holding is a read-down of the 1992 Validation Law (not a direct invalidation of the fee increases). Recommend adjusting the "on its own facts" sentence accordingly; the doctrinal "lasting effect" framing needs no change.

sources.confirms: Docket בג"ק 4562/92, case name ח"כ אליעזר זנדברג נ' רשות השידור, citation פ"ד נ(2) 793, year 1996, statutes at issue, and the decision on TV fees / price-stability law.

sources.publisher: VERSA / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Quotes Barak's Zandberg principle: 'it is better to achieve a reduction in the scope of a statute by interpretive means rather than... declaring a part of a statute void because it conflicts with the provisions of a Basic Law,' and 'a reasonable interpretation of a statute is preferable to a decision on the question of its constitutionality.'

cases.blurb_he: חוק רשות השידור (אישור תוקף אגרות רדיו וטלוויזיה), התשנ"ב-1992 — שהכנסת חוקקה כדי להכשיר רטרואקטיבית גביית אגרות שנפסלה. השופט ברק לא ביטל את החוק אלא קרא אותו בצמצום: דחה את תחולתו הרטרואקטיבית על קנסות פיגורים מטעמי קניין ואי-ענישה למפרע. כאן נטבעה הדוקטרינה — "מוטב לצמצם חוק בפרשנות מאשר לפסול חלקו" — שהפכה למנוע שבו בית משפט לא-נבחר מרוקן מתוכן את חקיקת הכנסת בלי לבטל מילה אחת מן הנוסח.
cases.blurb_en: The Broadcasting Authority (Validation of Radio and TV Fees) Law, 5752-1992 — which the Knesset passed to retroactively validate fee collection an earlier ruling had voided. Barak did not strike the law; he read it down, refusing to apply its retroactive reach to arrears fines on property-rights and no-penalty-in-arrears grounds. This is where the doctrine was minted — "better to narrow a statute by interpretation than to declare part of it void" — the engine by which an unelected court hollows out the elected legislature's law without voiding a single word of text.

cases.name: Rubinstein v. Minister of Defense
cases.statute: The blanket yeshiva-student ('Torato Omanuto') military-service deferment arrangement as administered by the Minister of Defense under the Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986 (i.e., the deferment granted by executive/administrative discretion rather than by primary legislation)
cases.doctrine: Primary arrangements doctrine (a fundamental, society-dividing matter must be set by Knesset primary legislation, not executive discretion); rule of law / legality principle; separation of powers
cases.effect_reduction: The Court did not strike a statute; instead it read the Defense Service Law's grant of ministerial discretion narrowly, holding that the sweeping, ever-growing deferment of full-time yeshiva students (about 8% of the draft cohort by 1997) is a 'primary arrangement' that the Minister of Defense had no authority to create administratively. By construing the minister's statutory discretion as not extending to a mass societal exemption, the Court gutted the legal basis on which decades of de facto exemptions had rested — converting an open-ended executive practice into something that could survive only if the Knesset enacted explicit primary legislation. The Court suspended the declaration of invalidity for 12 months to let the Knesset legislate, which led directly to the 2002 Tal Law (and thus to the later strike-downs).
cases.corrected_facts: No corrections needed; all key facts confirmed. Minor clarifying notes: (1) The decision date is December 9, 1998. (2) The case is joined HCJ 3267/97 + HCJ 715/98, both confirmed in the official court caption and Versa. (3) The follow-on statute is most precisely the "Deferral of Military Service for Yeshiva Students Law" — popularly the Tal Law, enacted 2002 after the Tal Committee (1999). The candidate's chain (1998 ruling -> 12-month suspension -> Tal Committee -> 2002 Tal Law -> later strike-downs) is accurate. (4) Characterization caveat for precision: the Court did not "strike a statute"; it held the Minister of Defense lacked authority to maintain the deferment arrangement administratively because granting a sweeping exemption to a large, ever-growing group (~8% of the cohort by 1997) is a "primary arrangement" reserved to the Knesset under the post-1992 Basic-Law framework — i.e., it read the Defense Service Law's grant of ministerial discretion as not extending to a mass societal exemption. This matches the claimed action of interpretive narrowing / read-down rather than facial invalidation of a statute.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project (Yeshiva University)
sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 3267/97 + HCJ 715/98; decided 9 Dec 1998; Barak P. opinion; held the Minister acted unlawfully maintaining the deferment without statutory authority; deferment is a 'primary arrangement' for the Knesset; 12-month suspension granted

1998 Rubinstein ruling: Minister lacked authority for sweeping exemption of large numbers of yeshiva students without explicit authorization by law; Knesset given one year to legislate, leading to the Deferment (Tal) Law

sources.publisher: Official Israeli Supreme Court site (supremedecisions.court.gov.il) — English verdict
sources.confirms: Official court file for HC 3267/97 / HC 715/98 Rubinstein et al. v. Minister of Defense

cases.blurb_he: חוק שירות ביטחון [נוסח משולב], התשמ"ו-1986: בלי לפסול אות אחת, פירש בית המשפט בצמצום את שיקול הדעת שהחוק העניק לשר הביטחון וקבע שדחיית השירות הגורפת לבחורי ישיבה ("תורתו אומנותו" — כ-8% ממחזור הגיוס) היא "הסדר ראשוני" שרק הכנסת מוסמכת לקבוע. כך רוקנו שופטים בלתי-נבחרים פרקטיקה בת עשורים מבסיסה החוקי, והשהו את ההכרזה 12 חודשים כדי לכפות על המחוקק הנבחר לחוקק.
cases.blurb_en: The Defense Service Law (5746-1986): without voiding a single word, the Court read the discretion the statute gave the Defense Minister down to nothing, holding that the sweeping yeshiva-student deferment ("Torato Omanuto" — some 8% of the draft cohort) was a "primary arrangement" only the Knesset may set. Unelected justices thus stripped a decades-old practice of its legal basis, suspending the ruling 12 months to force the elected legislature to act.

cases.name: Public Committee Against Torture in Israel v. Government of Israel
cases.statute: General Security Service (GSS/Shin Bet) interrogation authority — claimed under the general investigatory power (Criminal Procedure [Testimony] Ordinance, s.2(1)) combined with the necessity defense, Penal Law 5737-1977 s.34(11)
cases.doctrine: Principle of legality / requirement of express statutory authorization for infringement of liberty and dignity (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty); necessity (s.34(11)) is an ex post criminal defense, not an ex ante source of administrative authority.
cases.effect_reduction: Following the Landau Commission framework, the GSS treated the Penal Law necessity defense as a standing legal basis authorizing 'moderate physical pressure' (shaking, the shabah stress position, frog-crouch, etc.) as routine interrogation policy. The Court read this down to nothing operative: it held the general power to interrogate does NOT authorize physical means, and that the necessity defense cannot serve as advance authorization or as a basis for administrative directives — it can at most be raised after the fact by an individual interrogator facing prosecution. General directives permitting physical means must rest on express primary legislation, which did not exist. The Court thus stripped the executive of the legal cover it claimed it already had under existing statute, leaving the interrogation regime authority-less unless and until the Knesset legislated — substantially gutting the practical power the State asserted flowed from the existing legal framework.
cases.corrected_facts: Minor precision additions (not corrections; candidate is substantively accurate): (1) The full docket is HCJ 5100/94 consolidated with HCJ 4054/95, HCJ 6536/95, HCJ 5188/96, HCJ 7563/97, HCJ 7628/97, and HCJ 1043/99. The candidate's shorthand "HCJ 5100/94 (with consolidated petitions)" is correct. (2) Decided September 6, 1999, by an expanded panel led by President Aharon Barak. (3) The reporter citation is PD 53(4) 817 (the candidate's "ISrSC 53-4-817" in the Columbia source slug matches). The action characterization (interpretive_narrowing / read-down of the asserted statutory+necessity-defense authority) is accurate.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: HCJ 5100/94 (1999); GSS lacked authority to use physical interrogation methods; necessity defense (Penal Law s.34(11)) cannot serve as advance authorization, only as a post hoc defense; general directives for physical means must be rooted in express statutory authorization.

sources.publisher: HaMoked: Center for the Defence of the Individual
sources.confirms: HCJ 5100/94 Public Committee Against Torture in Israel et al. v. Government of Israel judgment text; ISA not authorized to employ physical means of pressure; banned shaking, shabah, frog crouch.
HCJ 5100/94, IsrSC 53(4) 817 (1999); Court held GSS not authorized to use physical means during interrogation; necessity defense available only ex post, not as authority.

cases.blurb_he: פקודת סדר הדין הפלילי (עדות) וסייג הצורך שבחוק העונשין שימשו את השב"כ כבסיס משפטי ל"לחץ פיזי מתון" בחקירות. בית המשפט לא ביטל אף הוראת חוק — אלא פירש אותן עד אפס: הסמכות לחקור אינה מתירה אמצעים פיזיים, וסייג הצורך הוא טענת הגנה בדיעבד ליחיד, לא הסמכה מראש. כך רוקנו שופטים בלתי נבחרים את מארג החקיקה שעליו נשען המנגנון, והותירוהו ללא סמכות עד שהכנסת תחוקק במפורש.
cases.blurb_en: The GSS leaned on the Criminal Procedure (Testimony) Ordinance and the Penal Law's necessity defense as legal cover for "moderate physical pressure" in interrogations. The Court voided no statute — it read them down to nothing: the power to interrogate authorizes no physical means, and necessity is only an after-the-fact defense for an individual, never advance authorization. Unelected justices thus hollowed out the legislative framework the regime relied on, leaving it powerless until the Knesset legislates expressly.

cases.name: CA 6024/97 Shavit v. Rishon LeZion Jewish Burial Society
cases.panel: President A. Barak, Justice M. Cheshin, Justice I. Englard (majority: Cheshin and Barak)
cases.statute: Burial-society bylaws under religious burial authority; engaging Right to Alternative Civil Burial Law, 5756-1996; Standard Contracts Law; Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty
cases.doctrine: Burial societies as bodies of public/private dual character subject to public-law norms; human dignity / freedom of conscience limit on religious burial rules
cases.effect_reduction: The burial society enforced a Hebrew-only inscription rule on tombstones. The Court invalidated the rule as a discriminatory standard-contract term, requiring societies to permit non-Hebrew inscriptions and Gregorian dates. Effect: a religiously-grounded burial regulation was read down / struck on dignity grounds, narrowing the operative reach of the religious burial authority's rule-making.
cases.corrected_facts: The docket, parties, year, and statutes are all confirmed. One scope refinement to the claim's framing: the dispute concerned engraving GREGORIAN-calendar dates of birth/death (in Latin numerals) ALONGSIDE Hebrew dates on the tombstone — not a blanket "Hebrew-only inscription" ban on all non-Hebrew text. The burial society's rabbi (Rabbi Breuer) had stopped permitting Gregorian dates after his appointment, reversing prior practice. The Court (majority Cheshin, Barak) held that Jewish burial societies have a dual public/private character and are therefore subject to public-law principles; it rejected the argument that the Right to Alternative Civil Burial Law, 5756-1996 implicitly overrode the prior Kestenbaum precedent line; and it ruled the family had the right to inscribe Gregorian dates, finding the prohibition harmed the relatives' sensibilities and human dignity. So "narrowing the operative reach of the religious burial authority's rule-making" on dignity / standard-contract grounds is an accurate characterization of the effect, with the precise subject matter being Gregorian dates rather than non-Hebrew inscriptions generally.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: CA 6024/97, 1999-07-06, Barak/Cheshin/Englard, Hebrew-only inscription rule invalidated on human-dignity grounds

cases.blurb_he: חוק הזכות לקבורה אזרחית חלופית, התשנ"ו-1996, חוקק כדי להסדיר חופש בחירה בקבורה — אך לא ביטל בעיני בג"ץ את שלטון חברות הקדישא היהודיות, שמנעו מבני משפחה לחרות על מצבה תאריכי לידה ופטירה לועזיים (בספרות לטיניות) לצד התאריך העברי. בית המשפט, ברוב של חשין וברק, לא פסל תקנון ולא חוק, אלא הכריז כי חברות הקדישא הן גוף דו-מהותי הכפוף לכללי המשפט הציבורי, "קרא בצמצום" את כלל הרב, ופירש את המגבלה ככזו הפוגעת בכבוד האדם ובתחושת הקרובים — וכך הפך פסיקה דתית-מנהלית לאות מתה מבלי לבטל את לשונה.
cases.blurb_en: The Right to Alternative Civil Burial Law, 5756-1996 was enacted to widen burial choice — yet the Court refused to read it as overriding the Jewish burial societies whose rabbi barred a family from engraving Gregorian birth/death dates (in Latin numerals) alongside the Hebrew dates on a tombstone. Voiding neither statute nor bylaw, the majority (Cheshin, Barak) instead declared the societies dual public/private bodies bound by public-law norms, read the rule down, and recast the prohibition as an unconstitutional affront to the relatives' dignity — an unelected court hollowing a religious burial authority's rule-making into a shell while leaving its text formally intact.

cases.name: Anonymous (Plonim, Lebanese detainees) v. Minister of Defense — "bargaining chips" case
cases.statute: Emergency Powers (Detentions) Law, 5739-1979, section 2
cases.doctrine: Statutory interpretation in light of the right to liberty and human dignity (purposive construction); administrative detention requires individual dangerousness; rejection of detention for negotiating leverage.
cases.effect_reduction: The executive had used section 2 of the Emergency Powers (Detentions) Law to hold Lebanese nationals — who had completed their criminal sentences and posed no current individual security threat — indefinitely as 'bargaining chips' to leverage the return of missing/captured Israeli soldiers (e.g., Ron Arad). The State read the statute's broad 'state security' detention ground to cover this bargaining purpose. In a further hearing reversing an earlier panel, the Court (Barak, 6-3) construed the Law to permit administrative detention ONLY where the detainee himself poses a danger to national security, holding there is no authority to detain someone from whom no danger emanates merely for his utility as leverage. The detainees were ordered released. This narrowed the operative reach the State had claimed for the detention power and foreclosed an entire category of executive detention the legislature's broad language had been stretched to cover.
cases.corrected_facts: Standard English case name on the official court site and Versa is "Does v. Ministry of Defense" (Hebrew: Plonim / Anonymous v. Minister of Defense). Decided April 12, 2000. Vote is correctly 6-3: majority = Barak (President), Levin (VP), Or, Mazza, Zamir, Dorner; dissent = Cheshin, Kedmi, Türkel. This was a Criminal Further Hearing (CrimFH / dnp) that reversed an earlier panel ruling which had upheld the detention (earlier hearing docket ADA 10/94). The candidate's substantive characterization is otherwise accurate.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: CrimFH 7048/97, decided 12 April 2000; statute = Emergency Powers (Detentions) Law 5739-1979, s.2; Barak majority held no authority to detain a person posing no danger, rejecting 'bargaining chip' detention; 6-3 vote; detainees released.

sources.publisher: HaMoked: Center for the Defence of the Individual
sources.confirms: CrimFH 7048/97 Anonymous v. Ministry of Defence judgment; Lebanese citizens held under s.2 of the Emergency Powers (Detentions) Law; detention as bargaining chips for missing security personnel held impermissible because the Law does not allow detaining a person who is not a threat.
FCrA 7048/97 Anonymous (Lebanese Citizens) v Minister of Defence of Israel, final decision 12 April 2000, Israel Supreme Court; bargaining-chip detention not authorized.

cases.blurb_he: הכנסת חוקקה את חוק סמכויות שעת חירום (מעצרים), התשל"ט-1979, וסעיף 2 שבו אִפשר לרשות מעצר מינהלי משיקולי ביטחון המדינה. בית המשפט לא ביטל אות אחת — בדיון נוסף (ברק, 6 נגד 3) הוא פירש את הסעיף בצמצום: מעצר מינהלי מותר רק כשהעצור עצמו מסכן את הביטחון, וכך רוקן מתוכן את החזקת אזרחים לבנונים כ"קלפי מיקוח" שהמדינה ביססה על אותו נוסח רחב. שלושה שופטים נבחרים מחקו מדיניות שלמה של המחוקק בלי לגעת בלשון החוק.
cases.blurb_en: The Knesset's Emergency Powers (Detentions) Law, 5739-1979, section 2, let the executive order administrative detention on state-security grounds. The Court voided no text — in a Further Hearing (Barak, 6-3) it read the section down to permit detention only where the detainee himself poses a danger, hollowing out the holding of Lebanese nationals as "bargaining chips" that the State had built on that same broad wording. Three unelected justices erased an entire executive policy without touching a word the legislature wrote.

cases.name: HCJ 953/01 Solodkin v. Beit Shemesh Municipality
cases.panel: Nine justices: Aharon Barak (President, author), Theodor Or, Eliahu Mazza, Mishael Cheshin, Jacob Turkel, Dorit Beinisch, Ayala Procaccia, Edmond E. Levy, Miriam Naor
cases.statute: Local Authorities (Special Authorization) Law, 5717-1956 — the statute empowering municipalities to enact by-laws restricting/prohibiting the sale of pork; and the municipal by-laws of Beit Shemesh, Carmiel and Tiberias issued under it.
cases.doctrine: Interpretation 'in conformity' with Basic Laws (freedom of occupation, dignity/conscience); proportionality and reasonableness as built-in limits on a statutory empowerment; balancing of competing values.
cases.effect_reduction: Rather than uphold the blanket municipal prohibitions the empowering statute on its face permitted, the 9-justice panel read the authorization down: it construed the power as requiring a proportionate, neighborhood-by-neighborhood balance between religious/national sensibilities and freedom of occupation/conscience, so a city-wide ban that left observant residents no practical access to pork is invalid. The by-laws were suspended and remanded for re-drafting under the new judicially-imposed criteria, narrowing the real-world reach of the prohibition power well below what the legislature's grant of authority appeared to allow.
cases.corrected_facts: No corrections needed. All candidate facts are accurate. Minor refinement: the panel of nine is named as Barak, Or, Mazza, Cheshin, Turkel, Beinisch, Procaccia, Levy, Naor (per the larc.cardozo record). Exact ruling date is 14 June 2004.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Dockets HCJ 953/01 etc., 14 June 2004, empowering statute (Local Authorities (Special Authorization) Law 1956) and pork by-laws, 9-justice panel, that the Court narrowed municipal discretion via a proportionality/balancing test requiring practical access rather than upholding blanket bans

cases.blurb_he: חוק הרשויות המקומיות (אישור מיוחד), התשי"ז-1956, הסמיך עיריות לאסור על מכירת בשר חזיר — וסמכות זו, כפשוטה, התירה איסור גורף. תשעה שופטים, בראשות ברק, לא פסלו את החוק אלא "קראו אותו במורד": פירשו את ההסמכה כמחייבת איזון מידתי שכונה-שכונה, התלו את חוקי העזר של בית שמש, כרמיאל וטבריה והחזירום לניסוח מחדש — וכך צמצמו בפועל את כוח האיסור הרבה מתחת לרוחב שהמחוקק הנבחר העניק, מבלי לבטל מילה מן הטקסט.
cases.blurb_en: The Local Authorities (Special Authorization) Law, 1956 empowered municipalities to ban pork sales — a grant that on its face permitted blanket prohibitions. Nine justices, led by Barak, struck nothing: they read the authorization down, construing it to require proportionate neighborhood-by-neighborhood balancing, suspended the Beit Shemesh, Carmiel and Tiberias by-laws and remanded them for redrafting — shrinking the power's real reach far below what the elected legislature had conferred, without voiding a word of the text.

cases.name: HCJ 2597/99 & 2859/99 Rodriguez-Tushbeim v. Minister of Interior
cases.panel: 11-justice panel: President Barak, Vice-President Emeritus Mazza, Vice-President Cheshin, Justice Emeritus Türkel, and Justices Beinisch, Rivlin, Procaccia, Levy, Grunis, Naor, Hayut. Vote 7-4 (majority: Barak, Mazza, Cheshin, Beinisch, Rivlin, Naor, Hayut; dissent: Türkel, Procaccia, Levy, Grunis).
cases.statute: Law of Return, 5710-1950, sec. 4B (חוק השבות)
cases.doctrine: Statutory interpretation of 'converted' broadened against the Interior Ministry's community-integration requirement
cases.effect_reduction: The Interior Ministry required converts to join and remain in the converting community to be recognized as Jews under the Law of Return. The Court (7-4, 11-justice panel) held it sufficient that the conversion took place in a recognized Jewish community abroad followed by immigration — recognizing non-Orthodox ('leap-frog') conversions and overriding the executive's narrowing gloss on sec. 4B.
cases.corrected_facts: Companion docket HCJ 2859/99 is correctly named "Makrina" (Makrina v. Minister of the Interior) — confirmed. DATE CAUTION: The candidate's year=2005 is supported by the authoritative Versa/Cardozo source (decision date May 31, 2005). However, the standard reporter citation appears as IsrSC 58(5) 412, and one secondary source rendered the date as "May 31, 2004." The 58(5) volume corresponds to the 2004 court year; the published decision date is best given as May 31, 2005 per Versa. Recommend citing "May 31, 2005 (IsrSC 58(5) 412)." TERMINOLOGY: "leap-frog conversion" is the candidate library's informal descriptor, not a phrase used by the Court; it accurately captures the Makrina fact pattern (non-Jewish lawful residents in Israel who studied Judaism in Israel and went abroad briefly for the conversion ceremony, then returned). The substantive action — rejecting the executive's narrowing gloss requiring converts to join/remain in the converting community, and recognizing non-Orthodox conversions abroad followed by immigration under sec. 4B of the Law of Return — is accurately characterized. Note HCJ 2597/99 itself was dismissed as moot (petitioner received citizenship while pending); the operative grant was on HCJ 2859/99 (Makrina) and the consolidated rulings.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Dockets 2597/99 & 2859/99, 2005-05-31, 11-justice panel, 7-4, Law of Return sec. 4B, conversion-abroad recognition against Ministry's community-integration requirement

cases.blurb_he: סעיף 4ב לחוק השבות, התש"י-1950, אינו מגדיר מיהו "שהתגייר", ומשרד הפנים דרש שמתגיירים יצטרפו לקהילה המגיירת וישתייכו אליה כדי שיוכרו כיהודים. בית המשפט העליון (הרכב 11 שופטים, 7-4, 2005) דחה את הפרשנות המצמצמת של הרשות המבצעת וקבע שדי בגיור בקהילה יהודית מוכרת בחו"ל ועלייה לאחריו — בית משפט בלתי-נבחר הכריע במחלוקת ערכית טעונה והרחיב את תחולת החוק על גיורים לא-אורתודוקסיים מעבר לקו שביקש המינהל הנבחר לשרטט.
cases.blurb_en: Section 4B of the Law of Return, 5710-1950, leaves "converted" undefined, and the Interior Ministry read it to require converts to join and belong to the converting community before recognition as Jews. The Supreme Court (11-justice panel, 7-4, 2005) rejected the executive's narrowing gloss and held that conversion in a recognized Jewish community abroad followed by immigration suffices — an unelected court settling a charged value question and stretching the statute to cover non-Orthodox conversions past the line the elected administration sought to draw.

cases.name: A v. State of Israel (CrimA 6659/06) — Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants Law
cases.statute: Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants Law, 5762-2002 ("Internment of Unlawful Combatants Law")
cases.doctrine: Constitutional review under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty; presumption of constitutionality; presumption of interpretive conformity with international humanitarian law; proportionality; reading-in of a strict evidentiary and individual-dangerousness requirement.
cases.effect_reduction: The Knesset designed the Law to permit indefinite incarceration of anyone fitting the broad statutory definition of 'unlawful combatant' (participation, direct OR indirect, in hostile acts, OR mere membership in a force perpetrating hostilities), backed by a strong section 7 presumption that release endangers state security — effectively allowing detention on group-membership grounds with the burden flipped onto the detainee. The Court upheld the Law facially but gutted that sweep by construing it to require proof of the detainee's OWN individual dangerousness, demanding 'significant' direct/indirect contribution to hostilities or meaningful organizational involvement (rejecting bare membership), insisting on 'clear and convincing administrative evidence,' and demoting the section 7 presumption to a secondary step that applies only AFTER individual threat is independently established. This converted a quasi-categorical group-detention regime into an individualized-threat regime — the legislature's intended reach was substantially narrowed without a formal strike.
cases.corrected_facts: Docket is real and consolidated: CrimA 6659/06 with companions 1757/07, 8228/07, and 3261/08 (decided June 11, 2008). Companion 1757/07 and 8228/07 are confirmed; the candidate's "(also cited CrimApp/CrimA 6659/06)" is fine — it is a criminal appeal (CrimA). Note CrimApp 8823/07 is a SEPARATE related detention case, not part of this consolidation — do not conflate it. Case name: Versa/Cardozo titles it "A v. State of Israel"; the official court translation renders the lead appellant as "Iyad v. State of Israel." The candidate's "Anonymous v. State of Israel" is an acceptable descriptive alias but the canonical English-source citation is "A v. State of Israel." Statute name: the candidate gives both "Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants Law" and "Internment of Unlawful Combatants Law, 5762-2002" — both are in circulation as translations of the same law; the official court translation and ICRC use "Internment/Incarceration of Unlawful Combatants Law, 5762-2002." All correct.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Docket CrimA 6659/06, decided 11 June 2008; statute = Internment of Unlawful Combatants Law 5762-2002; Court read down the definition to require 'significant' individual contribution and 'clear and convincing' evidence of individual dangerousness, with the section 7 presumption made secondary; interpretation in conformity with IHL and Basic Law.

sources.publisher: ICRC 'How does law protect in war?' casebook
sources.confirms: CrimA 6659/06 upheld the Law's constitutionality while holding detention is contingent on the arrested person's individual dangerousness to state security; preventative purpose; conformity with IHL.

sources.publisher: HaMoked: Center for the Defence of the Individual
sources.confirms: CrimApp 6659/06 A. et al. v. State of Israel judgment; detention contingent on individual dangerousness; constitutionality of the Unlawful Combatants Law addressed.

cases.blurb_he: חוק כליאתם של לוחמים בלתי-חוקיים, התשס"ב-2002, נכתב כדי לאפשר מעצר בלתי-מוגבל על בסיס השתייכות לארגון עוין, עם חזקה (סעיף 7) שמשחרורו של הכלוא מסכן את ביטחון המדינה. בית המשפט הותיר את החוק על כנו אך רוקן אותו מתוכן: הוא פירש אותו כדורש הוכחה של מסוכנות אישית ממשית בראיות מינהליות ברורות ומשכנעות, ודחק את החזקה לשלב משני — בית משפט בלתי-נבחר שהפך משטר מעצר קבוצתי שחוקקה הכנסת למשטר של איום פרטני.
cases.blurb_en: The Knesset's Internment of Unlawful Combatants Law, 5762-2002 was built to permit indefinite detention on group-membership grounds, backed by a section 7 presumption that release endangers the state. The Court left the statute formally standing but hollowed it out — reading it to require proof of the detainee's own individual dangerousness on clear and convincing administrative evidence, rejecting bare membership, and demoting the presumption to a secondary step. An unelected court quietly converted the legislature's categorical group-detention regime into an individualized-threat one, never voiding a word.

cases.name: Mana'e v. Tax Authority (מנאע נ' רשות המסים)
cases.panel: President Dorit Beinisch, Justice Hanan Melzer, Justice Uzi Fogelman (opinion authored by Fogelman)
cases.statute: Tax Authority practice of collecting tax debts at police security roadblocks, asserted under general collection/seizure powers in the Tax (Collection) Ordinance and related tax statutes
cases.action: interpretive_narrowing / read_down (executive power held ultra vires)
cases.doctrine: Administrative legality; an action infringing dignity, privacy, freedom of movement and due process requires explicit statutory authorization; general collection powers cannot be read to authorize debt-collection at security checkpoints
cases.effect_reduction: The Court read the tax-collection statutes narrowly and held the Tax Authority lacked explicit authority to collect debts at police roadblocks, declaring the practice unlawful for absence of authorization — barring the executive from leveraging security checkpoints for tax enforcement absent a clear statutory mandate
cases.corrected_facts: Statute should be named precisely: the core instrument is the Taxes (Collection) Ordinance, 1929 (פקודת המסים (גבייה)). Decided December 20, 2010. Panel: President Dorit Beinisch, Justice Hanan Melzer, Justice Uzi Fogelman; the operative opinion was written by Justice Fogelman (one fetched source garbled "Melzer" as "Maltz"). Reported as IsrSC 64(2) 479. The petitioner was Dr. Adel Mana'a, detained at a Jerusalem checkpoint and forced to pay ~7,400 NIS in cash. The Court's holding extended beyond roadblocks to public places generally (e.g., shopping malls).

sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 6824/07, 2010-12-20 ruling, Beinisch/Melcer/Fogelman panel, holding tax collection at police roadblocks lacked explicit statutory authority and infringed dignity/privacy/freedom of movement/due process

sources.publisher: HaMoked – Center for the Defence of the Individual
sources.confirms: HCJ 6824/07 Mana'e v. Tax Authority petition challenging the roadblock tax-collection practice

cases.blurb_he: רשות המסים נהגה לגבות חובות מס מאזרחים שנעצרו במחסומי משטרה — וגם בקניונים ובמקומות ציבוריים — בהסתמך על סמכויות הגבייה הכלליות שבפקודת המסים (גבייה), 1929. בלי לפסול אות אחת בחוק, פירש בית המשפט את סמכויות הגבייה בצמצום וקבע שאין בהן הסמכה מפורשת לגבות חוב במחסום ביטחוני; כך רוקנו שופטים בלתי-נבחרים מתוכן את כלי האכיפה שהמחוקק נתן לרשות, בלי לבטל את הטקסט.
cases.blurb_en: The Tax Authority routinely collected tax debts from citizens stopped at police checkpoints — and in malls and other public places — invoking the general collection powers of the Taxes (Collection) Ordinance, 1929. Without voiding a single word, the Court read those powers narrowly and held they contain no explicit authorization to collect a debt at a security roadblock; so unelected justices hollowed out an enforcement tool the legislature handed the executive, leaving the statutory text intact.

cases.name: Association for Civil Rights in Israel v. Israel Police ('Big Brother' / Communications Data Law)
cases.panel: Expanded 7-justice panel: President D. Beinisch (author), A. Grunis, E. Rivlin, M. Naor, E. Arbel, E. Hayut, H. Melcer
cases.statute: Criminal Procedure (Enforcement Powers — Communications Data) Law, 5768-2007 (חוק סדר הדין הפלילי (סמכויות אכיפה – נתוני תקשורת); 'חוק האח הגדול')
cases.doctrine: Constitutional-conformity construction; right to privacy (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty); proportionality
cases.effect_reduction: The Court declined to strike the 'Big Brother' communications-metadata law but, sitting as an expanded 7-justice panel, ordered key provisions read narrowly: police access to communications data is permitted only for a 'focused and concrete purpose' (e.g. a specific event with identified suspects/victims), NOT for general crime detection; and warrantless ('urgent') access is confined to extreme cases of saving life or grave circumstances where a court order genuinely cannot be obtained. This substantially curtailed the broad surveillance authority the Knesset enacted.
cases.corrected_facts: Minor corrections/refinements to candidate metadata (do not affect the substantive claim): (1) The petitioner/respondent caption in authoritative sources is "Association for Civil Rights in Israel v. Israel Police" (the candidate also names the Bar Association as co-petitioner and the Minister of Public Security as respondent; the lead caption is ACRI v. Israel Police). (2) The docket HCJ 3809/08 was joined/decided together with HCJ 9995/08. (3) Panel justice 'U. Grunis' in one source is the same as Asher (A.) Grunis. Year=2012, action=read_down, and statute are all correct.

sources.confirms: HCJ 3809/08; 7-justice expanded panel under Beinisch; law not struck but ordered interpreted narrowly — focused/concrete purpose only and warrantless access limited to extreme life-saving cases
28 May 2012 ruling rejecting petitions against the Communications Data Law while requiring narrowing construction

cases.blurb_he: חוק סדר הדין הפלילי (סמכויות אכיפה – נתוני תקשורת), התשס"ח-2007 – "חוק האח הגדול" שהכנסת חוקקה כדי לתת למשטרה גישה רחבה לנתוני תקשורת. הרכב מורחב של שבעה שופטים בראשות הנשיאה ביניש סירב לפסול אותו, אך פירש אותו בצמצום עד שהותיר ממנו קליפה: הגישה הוגבלה ל"תכלית ממוקדת וקונקרטית" בלבד ולא לאיתור עבריינות כללי, וגישה ללא צו הוצרה למקרי קיצון של הצלת חיים – וכך בית משפט בלתי-נבחר ריקן מתוכן את סמכות המעקב הרחבה שביקשה הכנסת הנבחרת, בלי לבטל מילה מן הטקסט.
cases.blurb_en: The Criminal Procedure (Enforcement Powers — Communications Data) Law, 5768-2007 — the "Big Brother" law the Knesset passed to grant police sweeping access to communications metadata. An expanded 7-justice panel under President Beinisch refused to strike it, yet read it so narrowly it left a shell: access was confined to a "focused and concrete purpose" rather than general crime detection, and warrantless access to extreme life-saving cases — an unelected court hollowing out the broad surveillance power the elected legislature intended, without voiding a single word of the text.

cases.name: HaMoked: Center for the Defense of the Individual v. Minister of Defense
cases.panel: Justice Elyakim Rubinstein (authoring), Justice Esther Hayut, Justice Noam Sohlberg
cases.statute: Regulation 119 of the Defence (Emergency) Regulations, 1945 (תקנה 119 לתקנות ההגנה (שעת חירום)) — punitive house demolitions
cases.doctrine: Construction 'in conformity with' Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty (preserved pre-Basic-Law legislation clause); proportionality; prohibition on collective punishment
cases.effect_reduction: Although the validity of Regulation 119 is shielded by the preservation-of-laws clause (so it could not be formally struck), the Court held the power MUST be construed according to Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty and exercised proportionately. It imposed an open list of proportionality subtests (offense severity, affected innocent family members, strength of evidence, whether sealing or partial demolition would suffice) — converting an unfettered military demolition power into a constrained, individualized one and barring disproportionate use as collective punishment.
cases.corrected_facts: Minor framing caveat (not disqualifying): the petition was DENIED — the Court upheld the State's authority to demolish under Regulation 119 as lawful under domestic and international law, treating its purpose as deterrence rather than punishment. The candidate's "interpretive_narrowing" characterization is nonetheless accurate as to what the Court did to the power itself: although Regulation 119 is shielded from formal invalidation by the preservation-of-laws clause (pre-Basic Law "law in force"), the Court held it "must be interpreted in accordance with the Basic Law, and must be exercised in a measured and proportionate manner," and may not be used disproportionately as collective punishment (prohibited under international law). The Court enumerated the proportionality factors the candidate lists: severity of the offenses; number/characteristics of those foreseeably affected (innocent residents); strength of the evidence and extent of other dwellers' involvement; and whether sealing or partial demolition of only the suspect's portion would suffice as a less harmful means. So the claim is accurate; it should just not be mis-read as the petitioners winning a strike-down.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 8091/14, date 31 Dec 2014, panel Rubinstein/Hayut/Sohlberg, holding that Reg. 119 must be construed per Basic Law and exercised proportionately with proportionality subtests
Court narrowed Reg. 119 via proportionality / construction rather than striking; exceptions and partial-demolition/sealing alternatives

cases.blurb_he: תקנה 119 לתקנות ההגנה (שעת חירום), 1945 — סמכות הריסת בתים עונשית — חוסה תחת שמירת הדינים ולכן לא ניתן היה לפסול אותה. במקום זאת קבע בית המשפט שיש לפרש את התקנה לאור חוק יסוד: כבוד האדם וחירותו ולהפעילה במידתיות, וכפה עליה מבחני־משנה (חומרת המעשה, פגיעה בבני משפחה חפים, עוצמת הראיות, האם אטימה או הריסה חלקית תספיק) — וכך הפך שופטים שלא נבחרו סמכות צבאית גורפת לכוח כבול ומפוקח, שאסור להפעילו כענישה קולקטיבית, מבלי לגעת בלשון התקנה.
cases.blurb_en: Regulation 119 of the Defence (Emergency) Regulations, 1945 — punitive house demolitions — is shielded by the preservation-of-laws clause, so the Court could not strike it. Instead, unelected justices held the power MUST be construed under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty and exercised proportionately, imposing subtests (severity of offense, harm to innocent family members, strength of evidence, whether sealing or partial demolition would suffice) — converting a sweeping military demolition power into a constrained, individualized one barred from use as collective punishment, all without touching a word of the text.

cases.name: בג"ץ 5239/11 אורי אבנרי ואח' נ' הכנסת (Avneri v. Knesset — Boycott Law)
cases.statute: Prevention of Harm to the State of Israel by means of Boycott Law, 5771-2011 (החוק למניעת פגיעה במדינת ישראל באמצעות חרם, תשע"א-2011) — the civil-tort provision, section 2.
cases.doctrine: Narrow construction / reading-down to limit the 'chilling effect' on freedom of expression, in conformity with Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty. Section 2(c) (exemplary/independent-of-damage compensation) was formally struck; sections 2(a)-(b) (the boycott tort) were upheld but read down rather than voided.
cases.effect_reduction: The Knesset designed a deterrent tort: anyone who knowingly publishes a public call to boycott Israel (or an area under its control) is liable, and section 2(c) expressly allowed a court to award compensation 'without proof of damage' — making it a punitive, low-threshold cause of action meant to chill boycott advocacy. The Court (Melcer, majority) struck 2(c) outright, then gutted what remained by reading the surviving tort down to ordinary tort principles: liability requires PROOF of actual damage AND a causal connection between the call and the damage, plus awareness of the reasonable possibility the call would lead to a boycott, with standing confined to the direct victim of the tort. By demanding real, provable, causally-linked damage, the Court neutralized the provision's deterrent bite — the realistic ability to sue a boycott advocate collapsed — substantially defeating the legislature's intent while leaving the text formally standing.
cases.corrected_facts: None. The docket, parties, year (2015, specifically 15 April 2015), statute name, the unanimous strike of section 2(c) (= Hebrew 2(ג)), and the read-down of the surviving tort (proof of actual damage, causal connection, awareness of reasonable possibility, direct-victim standing) are all accurately stated.

sources.publisher: VERSA / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 5239/11 (+ consolidated), 15 April 2015; section 2(c) voided; sections 2(a)/(b) upheld but read down by Melcer to require proof of actual damage, causal connection, awareness, and direct-victim standing.

sources.publisher: HaMoked – Center for the Defence of the Individual
sources.confirms: HCJ 5239/11 Avneri v. Knesset judgment; court left the Boycott Law standing except the section allowing damages without proof of harm, which was annulled; remedies under the boycott tort require proof of damage and causation.
2015 ruling struck section 2(c) (compensation even where no damages proven) and upheld remaining provisions; statute name and 2011 date.

cases.blurb_he: חוק החרם (התשע"א-2011) נועד להרתיע: כל קורא בפומבי לחרם על ישראל נושא באחריות, וסעיף 2(ג) התיר פיצוי "ללא הוכחת נזק". הכנסת חוקקה — ובית משפט בלתי-נבחר ביטל את סעיף 2(ג) וקרא את שאר העוולה כך שתחייב הוכחת נזק ממשי וקשר סיבתי, ובכך רוקן מתוכן את עוקץ ההרתעה והותיר את החוק קיים-להלכה אך מנוטרל-למעשה.
cases.blurb_en: The Boycott Law (5771-2011) was built to deter: anyone publicly calling to boycott Israel is liable, and section 2(c) allowed compensation "without proof of damage." The elected Knesset legislated — and an unelected court struck 2(c) outright, then read the surviving tort down to demand proof of actual damage plus causation, hollowing out its deterrent bite and leaving the statute standing in name while neutralized in fact.

cases.name: Conservative (Masorti) Movement & Movement for Progressive Judaism v. Be'er Sheva Religious Council
cases.statute: חוק שירותי הדת היהודיים [נוסח משולב], התשל"א-1971 — Jewish Religious Services (Consolidated Version) Law, 1971 (with the 2000 Prohibition of Discrimination Law and Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty)
cases.doctrine: Equality / prohibition of discrimination in publicly funded services; construing the religious-services statute against the religious councils' exclusionary use of public mikvaot
cases.effect_reduction: The religious councils had used their statutory control over publicly funded mikvaot (ritual baths) to bar Reform and Conservative movements from immersing converts — effectively extending the Orthodox conversion monopoly into physical infrastructure. The Court held that once the state establishes public mikvaot for public use including conversion, it cannot discriminate by denominational stream, and ordered Be'er Sheva (and, by extension, other councils permitting conversion immersion) to admit non-Orthodox converts including with their rabbinic tribunal present. This read the religious-services framework down so it could not be wielded to deny non-Orthodox access.
cases.corrected_facts: Decision date is 11 February 2016 (candidate year=2016 is correct). The official court PDF/TXT confirms the docket: supremedecisions.court.gov.il path "10/750/058" decodes to docket 5875/10. Minor naming note: the case is most commonly cited under the lead appellant "The Masorti (Conservative) Movement" (התנועה המסורתית); the Reform Movement (Movement for Progressive Judaism / IMPJ) is a co-appellant — the candidate's two-party name is accurate. One nuance worth flagging for precision: the Court framed the real respondent as the State of Israel and stated the ruling applies to all local/religious councils (not Be'er Sheva alone), which the candidate's "by extension" phrasing captures correctly.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Docket AAA 5875/10, decided 11 Feb 2016; statutes involved; holding that converts of private systems must be permitted to immerse in public Be'er Sheva mikveh and that other councils must find a similar solution
2016 ruling that state-run mikvaot cannot deny entry to Reform/Conservative conversions; Justices Rubinstein, Joubran, Naor

cases.blurb_he: חוק שירותי הדת היהודיים נתן למועצות הדתיות שליטה על מקוואות ציבוריים — והן ניצלו זאת כדי לחסום גיורי רפורמים וקונסרבטיבים. בלי לפסול אות אחת בחוק, פירש בית המשפט הלא-נבחר את ההסדר בצמצום: מקווה ציבורי שנפתח לטבילת גיור אסור בהפליה לפי זרם, וכך נגדע השימוש שהמועצות עשו בסמכותן החוקית להנצחת המונופול האורתודוקסי — לא רק בבאר שבע אלא בכל המועצות.
cases.blurb_en: The Jewish Religious Services Law gave religious councils control over public mikvaot — which they used to bar Reform and Conservative conversion immersions. Voiding nothing in the text, the unelected Court read the statute down: a public mikveh opened for conversion may not discriminate by denominational stream, hollowing out the councils' use of their statutory authority to entrench the Orthodox monopoly — binding not just Be'er Sheva but every council.

cases.name: Rogachova v. Ministry of the Interior
cases.statute: חוק השבות, התש"י-1950 — Law of Return, 1950 (sec. 4B definition of 'Jew' / conversion)
cases.doctrine: Purposive interpretation of 'conversion' under the Law of Return; recognition of 'recognized Jewish community' conversions; review of executive's restrictive reading
cases.effect_reduction: The Interior Ministry had administered the Law of Return so that only conversions performed by the official state (Orthodox) conversion system inside Israel counted for Return-Law recognition. The Court rejected this restrictive executive construction, holding that 'conversion' means conversion under the auspices of a recognized Jewish community per its established criteria — thereby recognizing private/independent Orthodox-community conversions performed in Israel (for those lawfully resident at the time) and overriding the Ministry's narrower gloss on the statute. The narrowing runs against the executive's intended limitation, not the statute's text, and laid groundwork for the 2021 non-Orthodox ruling.
cases.corrected_facts: Decision date is March 31, 2016 (year 2016 confirmed). Important nuance on outcome: the lead/named petitioner Martina Rogachova (HCJ 7625/06) actually LOST — her petition was denied because she underwent her Orthodox conversion (in Rabbi Karelitz's Bnei Brak rabbinical court, outside the state system) while residing in Israel UNLAWFULLY (her tourist visa had expired). The consolidated petitioners in HCJ 1594/11 and HCJ 1595/11 WON and were recognized as Jews for Law of Return purposes. So the recognition of private/independent Orthodox-community conversions applies to those lawfully resident at the time of conversion — the candidate's "for those lawfully resident at the time" qualifier is correct, but the framing of Rogachova as a successful petitioner should be avoided (she is the named-but-losing lead). The substantive legal holding in the candidate description is accurate.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Dockets HCJ 7625/06, 1594/11, 1595/11; decided 31 March 2016; holding that recognized-community Orthodox conversions in Israel qualify under the Law of Return, with the lawful-residence limitation
Full judgment text confirming dockets, President M. Naor, and the conversion-recognition holding

cases.blurb_he: חוק השבות, התש"י-1950: בלי לפסול אות אחת, פירשו השופטים את "גיור" שבסעיף 4ב כך שיכלול גם גיורי קהילה אורתודוקסית פרטית שנעשו בארץ (למי שישב בה כדין), ודחו את קריאתה המצמצמת של רשות ההגירה. ערכאה לא-נבחרת קבעה מחדש את משמעות המונח שבחוק — אף שהעותרת ששמה נישא בכותרת עצמה הפסידה.
cases.blurb_en: Law of Return, 1950: without voiding a word, the justices read section 4B's term "conversion" to cover private Orthodox-community conversions performed in Israel (for those lawfully resident), overriding the Interior Ministry's restrictive gloss. An unelected court reset what the statute's term means in practice — even as the lead petitioner whose name the case bears lost her own claim.

cases.name: בג"ץ 8101/15 צגטה נ' שר הפנים / HCJ 8101/15 Tsegeta (Tzegete) v. Minister of Interior
cases.statute: Entry into Israel Law / Prevention of Infiltration Law detention powers, as applied to the government's 'voluntary departure' arrangement deporting Eritrean and Sudanese asylum seekers to unnamed third countries (Rwanda/Uganda)
cases.doctrine: Read-down / interpretation in conformity with the arrangement's own terms and international-law non-refoulement constraints; the third-country agreements permit only consensual transfer
cases.effect_reduction: The Court did NOT strike the deportation arrangement or the underlying detention powers. Instead it read them down: because the third-country agreements permit only consensual transfer, the state may not detain a person solely for refusing to leave, nor forcibly deport them. This interpretive narrowing hollowed out the enforcement teeth — the government could no longer use indefinite detention as coercion and could only remove those who 'consented,' effectively freezing the forced-deportation program against the executive's clear intent to compel departures.
cases.corrected_facts: Decision date is 28 August 2017 (the candidate's year=2017 is correct). The matter reached the Supreme Court as an administrative appeal (originating from District Court Administrative Appeal 5126-07-15 / Zegete v. Ministry of Interior), and is commonly cited in the HCJ/Bagatz form (HCJ 8101/15). Sourcing caveat: the candidate listed the IDI URL https://en.idi.org.il/articles/3938 — that page is about a DIFFERENT case (HCJ 8665/14 Desete v. Minister of Interior, Aug. 2015), not 8101/15. The IDI page that actually treats the third-country/voluntary-departure issue is https://en.idi.org.il/articles/3735. The Hotline source (the other provided source) is correct and directly supports the holding.

sources.confirms: HCJ 8101/15 Tsegeta v. Minister of Interior, Aug 28, 2017; court held no statute struck but forced deportation and detention-for-refusal prohibited because agreement allows only consensual deportation; Dec 30, 2015 injunction froze the plan
Legal challenges to removal of asylum seekers to third states; consent requirement limiting deportation

cases.blurb_he: סמכויות המעצר וההרחקה שבחוק הכניסה לישראל וחוק למניעת הסתננות לא נפסלו — בית המשפט פירש אותן בצמצום עד שרוקנו מתוכן: מאחר שההסכמים עם המדינות השלישיות (רואנדה/אוגנדה) מתירים העברה בהסכמה בלבד, אסור לעצור מבקש מקלט אריתריאי או סודני בשל סירובו לעזוב ואסור לגרשו בכפייה. כך הקפיאו שופטים שלא נבחרו את תוכנית הגירוש שאליה כיוונה המדינה — בלי לבטל ולו מילה אחת.
cases.blurb_en: The detention and removal powers under the Entry into Israel Law and Prevention of Infiltration Law were not struck — the Court read them down until they were hollow: because the third-country agreements (Rwanda/Uganda) permit only consensual transfer, the state may neither detain an Eritrean or Sudanese asylum seeker for refusing to leave nor deport him by force. Unelected judges thus froze the forced-departure program the executive had built, without voiding a single word.

cases.name: שי גיני ואח' נ' הרבנות הראשית לישראל (Further Hearing — Dnagatz 5026/16 on HCJ 6494/14)
cases.statute: חוק איסור הונאה בכשרות, התשמ"ג-1983 — Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 1983, sec. 3(a)
cases.doctrine: Statutory interpretation in conformity with freedom of occupation (Basic Law: Freedom of Occupation); narrow construction of the kashrut-certification monopoly to avoid unconstitutionality
cases.effect_reduction: The Rabbinate's statutory monopoly on kashrut representation (affirmed in the prior HCJ 6494/14 panel) was substantially gutted. In the Further Hearing, the Court read sec. 3(a) to create a 'third gate': a food business WITHOUT a Rabbinate certificate may now lawfully present truthful factual information about the kashrut standards it observes and which body (even a non-Rabbinate/private body) supervises it — explicitly including a statement that it holds no Rabbinate certificate — so long as it does not use the word 'kosher' (כשר) and does not mislead. This carved out the legal space for the 'alternative/private kashrut' movement (e.g., Hashgacha Pratit) that the Rabbinate's reading of the statute was meant to foreclose.
cases.corrected_facts: Lead opinion authored by President Miriam Naor (decided 12 Sept 2017). The candidate's "substantially gutted" overstates the holding: the Rabbinate's exclusive authority over kashrut *certification* and over the word "כשר/kosher" was PRESERVED (the 2016 panel's constitutional holding stood). What the Further Hearing did was NARROW/READ-DOWN sec. 3(a) to carve out a "third gate" of truthful disclosure — a business without a Rabbinate certificate may present truthful factual info about the standards it observes and who (incl. a private body) supervises it, including an explicit statement that it holds no Rabbinate certificate, so long as it does not use the word "כשר" and does not mislead. Note also: the ruling was by majority with a conditional/two-year sunset tied to reform of the kashrut-supervisor employment arrangement; Justice Solberg dissented on scope. Statute/section (Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 5743-1983, sec. 3(a)) is correct.

sources.confirms: Official judgment text of Dnagatz 5026/16, decided 12 Sept 2017
Docket Dnagatz 5026/16, parties, date 12.09.2017, and the 'third gate'/truthful-representation holding narrowing sec. 3(a)
Background of the Gini/Vadai petition over private kashrut certification and the underlying Kashrut Fraud Law dispute

cases.blurb_he: חוק איסור הונאה בכשרות, התשמ"ג-1983 (סע' 3(א)): מבלי לפסול אות אחת, פירשו השופטים את החוק "בצמצום" עד שהותירו מן ההלכה שביקשה הכנסת לעגן רק קליפה. מונופול הרבנות על המילה "כשר" נשמר — אך נחצב "פתח שלישי": עסק ללא תעודת רבנות רשאי למסור מידע אמת על מי שמפקח עליו (גם גוף פרטי) ולהצהיר שאין בידו תעודה, ובלבד שלא יאמר "כשר". כך פתחו הכשרות הפרטית שהחוק נועד לחסום.
cases.blurb_en: Kashrut (Prohibition of Fraud) Law, 1983 (sec. 3(a)): without voiding a single word, the justices "read down" the statute until the rule the Knesset enacted was left a near-shell. The Rabbinate's monopoly over the word "kosher" survived — but the Court carved a "third gate": an uncertified business may now state truthful facts about who supervises it (even a private body) and admit it holds no Rabbinate certificate, so long as it never says "kosher." Exactly the private-kashrut path the law meant to foreclose.

cases.name: HCJ 6494/14 & HCJ-FH (דנג"ץ) 5026/16 Gini v. Chief Rabbinate (גיני נ' הרבנות הראשית)
cases.panel: Expanded 7-justice panel (Further Hearing / דיון נוסף): President M. Naor, Deputy President A. Rubinstein, Deputy President S. Jubran, Justice E. Hayut, Justice H. Melcer, Justice N. Solberg, Justice A. Shaham
cases.statute: Prohibition of Kashrut Fraud Law, 5743-1983, sec. 3(a) (חוק איסור הונאה בכשרות)
cases.doctrine: Narrowing/conforming interpretation of penal statute; freedom of occupation and free speech / commercial-truth values used to read down the prohibition
cases.effect_reduction: The Rabbinate read sec. 3(a) to bar any non-certified business from making ANY kashrut representation. The expanded panel narrowed it: a restaurant without a Rabbinate certificate may not call itself 'kosher,' but MAY present truthful information about the standards it keeps and how supervision is conducted, including an explicit statement that it has no Rabbinate certificate. This gutted the Rabbinate's enforcement reach and opened the door to alternative/private kashrut supervision being described to consumers.
cases.corrected_facts: Panel was a 7-justice expanded panel (Naor, Rubinstein, Jubran, Hayut, Melcer, Solberg, Shaham), as expected for a Further Hearing — candidate did not specify panel composition. No factual corrections to the candidate's claim or characterization are needed.

sources.confirms: Dockets 6494/14 and 5026/16, dates, sec. 3(a) statute, narrowed-interpretation holding allowing truthful disclosure without 'kosher'
Further Hearing 5026/16 dated 12/09/2017; business may present truthful representation of standards and supervision incl. clarification it has no kashrut certificate
2017 High Court ruling narrowing Rabbinate kashrut monopoly / truthful-disclosure principle

cases.blurb_he: המחוקק אסר בחוק איסור הונאה בכשרות, התשמ"ג-1983 (סע' 3(א)) על הצגת מצג כשרות ללא תעודה מהרבנות, והרבנות אכפה זאת כמונופול גורף. הרכב מורחב של שבעה שופטים בדיון נוסף לא ביטל אות אחת מן הסעיף — אך פירש אותו בצמצום כך שבית עסק רשאי למסור מידע אמת על הסטנדרטים שהוא שומר ועל אופן הפיקוח, ואף להבהיר שאין לו תעודת הרבנות, ובלבד שלא יכנה עצמו "כשר"; כך רוקנו שבעה שופטים בלתי-נבחרים את האכיפה ואת המונופול שהמחוקק ביקש, מבלי לגעת בלשון החוק.
cases.blurb_en: The Knesset's Prohibition of Kashrut Fraud Law, 1983 (sec. 3(a)) barred any kashrut representation without a Rabbinate certificate, and the Rabbinate enforced it as a sweeping monopoly. An expanded seven-justice panel in a Further Hearing voided not a single word — it read the section down so that an uncertified eatery may truthfully state which standards it keeps and how it is supervised, and even disclose that it holds no Rabbinate certificate, so long as it does not call itself "kosher"; thus seven unelected justices hollowed out the enforcement reach and monopoly the elected legislature intended, while leaving the text untouched.

cases.name: Ben Meir v. Prime Minister (בן מאיר נ' ראש הממשלה)
cases.panel: President E. Hayut, Deputy President H. Melcer, Justice N. Sohlberg
cases.statute: Government's emergency authorization (per Sec. 7(b)(6) of the General Security Service Law, 5762-2002) plus emergency regulations (תקנות לשעת חירום) under Basic Law: The Government, deploying the Shin Bet (GSS) to track cell-phone location data of COVID-19 patients and their contacts
cases.doctrine: Emergency powers must be temporary and subject to the 'primary arrangements' principle; a significant infringement of privacy by an unconventional security tool may be sustained only briefly and, if continued, must be anchored in primary Knesset legislation rather than executive/cabinet authorization
cases.effect_reduction: The Court did not formally void the measure but held that continued GSS surveillance could not rest on the cabinet's administrative authorization/emergency mechanism; it set a short deadline and required the Government to begin a full primary-legislation process in the Knesset (with parliamentary oversight) to keep using the tool — forcing the executive's emergency program into the legislature and narrowing it to genuinely exceptional, time-limited use
cases.corrected_facts: Candidate is accurate. Clarifications: (1) The full consolidated petitions are HCJ 2109/20 (Shachar Ben Meir), 2135/20 (Association for Civil Rights in Israel), and 2141/20 (Adalah) — all confirmed. (2) The operative FINAL judgment forcing primary legislation was issued April 26, 2020. There was an earlier INTERIM order (March 19, 2020) that conditioned continued use on the Knesset establishing oversight committees; the candidate's described action (deadline + mandate to begin full primary-legislation process) corresponds to the April 26, 2020 final judgment. (3) Panel: Hayut, Melcer, Sohlberg.

sources.confirms: Docket, 2020-04-26 ruling date, Hayut-authored opinion, holding that continued GSS COVID tracking required primary Knesset legislation and could rest on the emergency mechanism only temporarily
Primary judgment text for HCJ 2109/20
Court froze GSS tracking absent a law; 'a law is needed' to continue

cases.blurb_he: הממשלה הסמיכה את השב"כ למעקב סלולרי אחר חולי קורונה ומגעיהם מכוח סעיף 7(ב)(6) לחוק השב"כ ותקנות שעת חירום לפי חוק-יסוד: הממשלה — והבג"ץ לא ביטל את הכלי במפורש, אלא קבע שהוא אינו יכול להישען על הסמכה מנהלית-ממשלתית: בית משפט בלתי-נבחר תחם את התוכנית בלוח זמנים קצר וחייב את הרשות המבצעת לפרק את מנגנון החירום שלה ולהעבירו לחקיקה ראשית בכנסת — וכך רוקן את ההסדר התקנתי הקיים מתוקף, גם בלי למחוק את לשונו.
cases.blurb_en: The Government authorized the Shin Bet to track COVID patients' and contacts' cell-phone location data under Sec. 7(b)(6) of the GSS Law and emergency regulations issued under Basic Law: The Government — and the unelected Court, without formally voiding the tool, held it could no longer rest on cabinet/administrative authorization, set a short deadline, and forced the executive to dismantle its own emergency mechanism and move the program into primary Knesset legislation, draining the existing regulatory arrangement of effect without striking its text.

cases.name: Secular Forum Association v. Minister of Health (עמותת הפורום החילוני נ' שר הבריאות)
cases.panel: Justices Neal Hendel, Uzi Vogelman, Ofer Grosskopf (Hendel partially dissented on the statutory-authority ground)
cases.statute: Hospital management directives/orders barring visitors from bringing chametz (leavened food) into hospitals during Passover, purportedly grounded in the Public Health Ordinance (פקודת בריאות העם) and the Patient's Rights Law (חוק זכויות החולה)
cases.action: struck_down (administrative directive) / interpretive_narrowing of enabling power
cases.doctrine: Principle of administrative legality (חוקיות המינהל); a fundamental-rights-infringing measure requires explicit statutory authorization; the enabling ordinance contains no clear and explicit authority to bar chametz or to search visitors' belongings on kashrut grounds, and security personnel's powers are limited to security purposes
cases.effect_reduction: The Court held the chametz ban and the accompanying bag-searches lacked any statutory source and were therefore invalid, narrowing the Public Health Ordinance and hospital-director powers so they could not be read to authorize enforcing kashrut — gutting the practical chametz prohibition hospitals had imposed
cases.corrected_facts: Characterization is accurate but slightly narrow: the ruling rested on TWO grounds, not just the statutory-source ground. (1) Lack of statutory authority — the Court found no clear authorization in Section 9 of the Patient's Rights Law and held that Section 33(h) of the Public Health Ordinance (1940) empowered only the Health Ministry Director-General, not individual hospitals, to regulate food; (2) Infringement of patients'/visitors' fundamental rights to autonomy, dignity, and freedom from religion. The candidate's 'lacked any statutory source / narrowed the enabling power' framing matches ground (1) and is correct, but omits the parallel rights-based ground. The companion petition was filed by Adalah; primary sources (Versa, Hebrew Wikipedia) cite it as HCJ 2240/18, matching the candidate (one secondary search snippet loosely referenced '1710/18 or 2240/18', but authoritative sources use 2240/18).

sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 1550/18, 2020-04-30 date, Fogelman/Grosskopf/Hendel panel, holding that the chametz ban lacked explicit statutory authority and that the Public Health Ordinance/Patient's Rights Law did not authorize it; searches by security personnel ultra vires

cases.blurb_he: בלי לפסול אות אחת בחקיקה, בית המשפט פירש את פקודת בריאות העם (1940) ואת חוק זכויות החולה כך שירוקנו מתוכן: סעיף 33(ח) לפקודה מסמיך רק את מנכ"ל משרד הבריאות — לא בתי חולים בודדים — להסדיר מזון, ובסעיף 9 לחוק זכויות החולה אין כל הסמכה לאסור חמץ או לחטט בתיקי מבקרים. שלושה שופטים בלתי-נבחרים הפכו צו מנהלי אוכף-כשרות לאות מתה ופוסלים אותו גם כפגיעה באוטונומיה, בכבוד ובחופש מדת — וכך מאיינים בפרשנות מדיניות שהמערכת ביקשה לכפות.
cases.blurb_en: Without voiding a single line of legislation, the Court read the Public Health Ordinance (1940) and the Patient's Rights Law into a shell: Section 33(h) empowers only the Health Ministry's Director-General — not individual hospitals — to regulate food, and Section 9 of the Patient's Rights Law grants no authority to ban chametz or search visitors' bags. Three unelected justices turned a kashrut-enforcing administrative directive into a dead letter, also striking it as an infringement of autonomy, dignity and freedom from religion — gutting by interpretation a policy the system meant to impose.

cases.name: בג"ץ 5555/18 Hasson v. Knesset (Nation-State Basic Law)
cases.statute: Basic Law: Israel – The Nation State of the Jewish People (2018), esp. Section 7 (development of Jewish settlement) and Section 1(c) (exclusive Jewish national self-determination)
cases.doctrine: Constitutional interpretation in harmony with the other Basic Laws and the Jewish-AND-democratic character of the state; Knesset as constituent authority is not all-powerful; equality read into the Basic Law
cases.effect_reduction: This is a textbook interpretive evisceration. The Knesset enacted a Basic Law whose plain text elevates 'Jewish settlement' to a constitutional national value, omits any mention of equality, and demotes Arabic from official to 'special status' — widely understood as a constitutional license to prefer Jews in land/housing allocation. Rather than strike it (10-1, only Justice Karra would have voided parts), the Court upheld the law but gutted its operative discriminatory bite: President Hayut held Section 7 must be read 'in a manner consistent with' Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty, so the value of Jewish settlement 'did not necessarily imply harm to equality' and an egalitarian land-allocation policy can be pursued; the State conceded, and the Court adopted, that Section 7 'is not intended to legalize the discrimination and exclusion of non-Jews from state lands.' The Court further held the Basic Law 'does not negate Israel's character as a democratic state.' Net effect: the legislature's apparent intent to constitutionally entrench Jewish preference in settlement/land was neutralized — the clause was left standing as a near-symbolic declaration stripped of the power to authorize discrimination.
cases.corrected_facts: Lead docket and case name confirmed: HCJ 5555/18 Akram Hasson v. Knesset (sometimes transliterated "Hason"). This was the lead docket among 15 consolidated petitions challenging Basic Law: Israel – The Nation State of the Jewish People; judgment delivered July 8, 2021. The bench was 11 justices, not the candidate's loosely implied count; vote was 10-1, with Justice George Karra (Kara) the sole dissenter who would have voided Sections concerning Jewish settlement, exclusive Jewish self-determination, and the Hebrew/Arabic language status. The candidate's substantive characterization is accurate and well-supported: President Hayut held Section 7 must be read consistently with sections 2 and 4 of Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty; that "the value of Jewish settlement did not necessarily imply harm to equality"; and that an egalitarian land-allocation policy may be pursued. The Court adopted a "harmonizing interpretation" treating the contested clauses (incl. 1(c) and 7) as declaratory and not authorizing discrimination/exclusion of non-Jews from state lands, and held the law does not negate Israel's democratic character. One nuance to flag if quoting verbatim: the precise wording "is not intended to legalize the discrimination and exclusion of non-Jews from state lands" reflects the substance the Court/State adopted but should be attributed to the judgment summary (Adalah translation / LOC) rather than presented as a sealed direct quote without that sourcing.

sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 5555/18, decided July 8 2021, 11-justice panel, Hayut majority, Karra dissent; Court interpreted the Basic Law consistent with other Basic Laws; Section 7 read so Jewish settlement does not imply harm to equality and egalitarian land allocation is permissible
Court's reading that Section 7 'is not intended to legalize the discrimination and exclusion of non-Jews from state lands,' as clarified by the State; law upheld but read down
10-1 ruling July 2021, Karra sole dissenter; Hayut: the Basic Law 'does not negate Israel's character as a democratic state'
