cases.blurb_he: חוק־יסוד: ישראל – מדינת הלאום של העם היהודי (2018), שהעלה את "פיתוח ההתיישבות היהודית" לערך חוקתי והשמיט כל אזכור של שוויון. בית המשפט (היוט, 10 מול 1) לא פסל אף סעיף, אלא "פירש" את סעיף 7 בהרמוניה עם כבוד האדם וחירותו — וקבע שאין בו היתר להפליה — כך שאחד עשר שופטים בלתי־נבחרים רוקנו מתוכן את מה שהכנסת הנבחרת ביקשה לעגן, והותירו את הסעיף כהצהרה ריקה מכוח.
cases.blurb_en: Basic Law: Israel – The Nation State of the Jewish People (2018) elevated "development of Jewish settlement" to a constitutional value and omitted equality entirely. The Court (Hayut, 10-1) struck nothing — it instead read Section 7 "in harmony" with Human Dignity and Liberty, holding it authorizes no discrimination, so eleven unelected justices hollowed out what the elected Knesset sought to entrench, leaving the clause a power-stripped declaration.

cases.name: Reform/Conservative Conversions in Israel (consolidated petitions) v. Minister of the Interior
cases.statute: חוק השבות, התש"י-1950 — Law of Return, 1950 (recognition of conversion for citizenship)
cases.doctrine: Purposive/equality interpretation of the Law of Return; judicial intervention after prolonged legislative non-action; non-discrimination between conversions performed in Israel vs. abroad
cases.effect_reduction: For decades the executive recognized non-Orthodox conversions performed ABROAD but not those performed IN ISRAEL, preserving the de facto Orthodox monopoly on Israeli conversion for Law-of-Return purposes. In an 8-1 decision (Chief Justice Hayut), the Court held the state could not justify recognizing an identical non-Orthodox conversion abroad while refusing one performed in Israel, and recognized Reform and Conservative conversions performed in Israel for the Law of Return. The Court expressly noted it had long deferred to let the Knesset legislate, and intervened only after ~15 years of legislative inaction — reading the statute against the administrative monopoly the religious establishment intended to preserve.
cases.corrected_facts: Scope clarification (not a contradiction): the March 1, 2021 ruling recognized non-Orthodox conversions performed in Israel for the Law of Return — covering Reform and Conservative conversions AND private/non-state Orthodox conversions performed outside the state conversion system. The candidate's Reform/Conservative framing is the accurate headline holding; the non-state-Orthodox component is an additional part of the same ruling. The dissenting justice was Noam Sohlberg (he agreed with the legal conclusion but would have allowed the Knesset more time). Decision date: March 1, 2021. Consolidated with related dockets including HCJ 11015/05.

sources.confirms: 1 March 2021 ruling recognizing Reform/Conservative conversions in Israel for the Law of Return; 8-1; CJ Hayut quote; Knesset failure to legislate for 15 years
8-1 decision; Barak-Erez note that the Court refrained earlier to allow legislation; recognition under the Law of Return

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 11013/05 as the consolidated petition pending on in-Israel non-Orthodox conversion recognition (referenced in Rogachova as the case where the issue would be decided)

cases.blurb_he: חוק השבות נותר על מכונו — אך בית המשפט (8 מול 1, הנשיאה חיות) רוקן מתוכן את המונופול שביקש הממסד לשמר: לאחר כ-15 שנות אי-חקיקה, פסקו שופטים שלא נבחרו כי המדינה אינה יכולה להכיר בגיור רפורמי או קונסרבטיבי שנעשה בחו"ל ולפסול גיור זהה שנעשה בישראל — והכירו בגיורים הלא-אורתודוקסיים (וכן בגיורים אורתודוקסיים פרטיים מחוץ למערך הממלכתי) לצורך חוק השבות, בניגוד לכוונה לשמר את הבלעדיות האורתודוקסית.
cases.blurb_en: The Law of Return was left textually intact — but the Court (8-1, CJ Hayut) hollowed out the monopoly the religious establishment sought to preserve: after some 15 years of Knesset inaction, unelected justices held the state could not recognize a Reform or Conservative conversion performed abroad yet reject an identical one performed in Israel, recognizing non-Orthodox (and private non-state Orthodox) conversions done in Israel for the Law of Return — against the intended Orthodox exclusivity.

cases.name: Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. The Knesset (Incapacitation Law)
cases.statute: Amendment No. 12 to Basic Law: The Government (the "Incapacitation Law"), enacted March 2023 — limited the grounds for declaring a sitting PM incapacitated to physical/mental ill-health only, abolishing court-recognized "legal incapacitation" (e.g. via criminal proceedings / conflict-of-interest violations).
cases.doctrine: Misuse of constituent power / 'personal' (ad hominem) constitutional amendment; interpretive postponement of entry-into-force (the 'Tiberias/Deri-style' enabling-interpretation technique) used to neutralize a Basic-Law amendment without formally invalidating it.
cases.effect_reduction: The Court did NOT formally strike the amendment. By a 6-5 majority it held the amendment was a misuse of the Knesset's constituent power because it was a 'personal' amendment tailored to benefit PM Netanyahu. Instead of voiding it, the Court 'cured the defect' by interpreting the amendment so that it does not apply to the sitting Knesset/government and takes effect only from the NEXT Knesset and election cycle. This postponement gutted the amendment's entire real-world purpose — shielding Netanyahu from a legal-incapacitation declaration — while leaving the text formally intact. A classic read-down/postponement-by-interpretation rather than a strike.
cases.corrected_facts: Refinements (no substantive errors in the candidate): (1) Decision date is January 3, 2024 — the candidate's year=2024 is correct, but if a month is ever stated it should be January 2024, not later. (2) The panel was 11 justices; the majority was 6 of 11, i.e., the "6-5" framing is accurate. (3) The petition was consolidated — HCJ 2412/23 (Movement for Quality Government) was joined with HCJ 2466/23 (MK Oded Forer / Yisrael Beiteinu). 2412/23 is the correct lead docket. (4) NOTE — do not confuse this with HCJ 5658/23 (the Reasonableness Standard case, Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary, also styled "Movement for Quality Government v. Knesset," decided the same day, Jan 1/3 2024), which the Court DID strike down. The Incapacitation case (2412/23) was read-down/postponed, not struck — exactly as the candidate states. The mechanism: the amendment was held a personal amendment / abuse of constituent power, cured by interpreting it to take effect only from the 26th Knesset onward.

sources.confirms: 6-5 ruling that the incapacitation amendment was a 'personal' misuse of constituent power; Court postponed its entry into force by interpretation rather than striking it, rendering the unlawful personal aspect largely inapplicable; decided in the first week of January 2024.

Eleven-justice bench, 6-5, declared the Basic Law: The Government incapacitation amendment a misuse of constituent power for personal aims and postponed its activation until after the next elections rather than annulling it; ruling Jan 3, 2024.

Explainer identifying HCJ docket/proceedings (11-justice panel, Sept 27, 2023 hearing) and previewing the 'enabling interpretation' postponing entry into force to the next Knesset, citing the Tiberias-5 precedent.

cases.blurb_he: תיקון 12 לחוק־יסוד: הממשלה ("חוק ההשבתה"), שצמצם את עילות ההכרזה על ראש ממשלה כמנוע־כהונה למצב בריאותי בלבד, נחקק כדי למגן את נתניהו. בית המשפט, ברוב 6-5, לא ביטל את התיקון אלא קבע שהוא ניצול לרעה של סמכות מכוננת — תיקון "פרסונלי" — ו"ריפא" אותו בפרשנות הקובעת שיחול רק מהכנסת הבאה, וכך רוקן את כל תכליתו בלי לגעת בלשונו. ערכאה לא־נבחרת מותירה את דבר המחוקק כקליפה ריקה.
cases.blurb_en: Amendment No. 12 to Basic Law: The Government (the "Incapacitation Law") narrowed the grounds for declaring a sitting PM incapacitated to ill-health alone, tailored to shield Netanyahu. The Court (6-5) did not strike it but held it a "personal" misuse of constituent power, "curing" it by interpretation so it applies only from the next Knesset — gutting its entire real-world purpose while leaving the text intact. An unelected bench reduces the legislature's enactment to an empty shell.

cases.name: Ressler v. Minister of Defence (רסלר נ' שר הביטחון) — HCJ 910/86
cases.panel: Shamgar P., Ben-Porat D.P., Barak J. (three-justice panel)
cases.statute: Yeshiva-student military-service deferment arrangement issued under the Defense Service Law (administrative deferment of full-time yeshiva students, 'Torato Umanuto')
cases.doctrine: Reasonableness / abuse of administrative discretion; justiciability and standing of public petitioners ('quantity becomes quality' threshold)
cases.effect_reduction: The Court (after reversing earlier standing/justiciability holdings) held the deferment was within the Defense Minister's authority and denied the petition on the merits — but expressly warned that once deferred yeshiva students grow to a 'significant portion' of conscription candidates, the deferment would become unreasonable and would have to be struck down. This installed a judicially-set ceiling over an arrangement the political branches intended to leave open-ended, converting an executive prerogative into something the Court reserved power to void.
cases.corrected_facts: Citation: 42(2) PD 441 (1988). Decision date 12 June 1988. Panel: President Shamgar, Deputy President Ben-Porat, Justice Barak (opinion by Barak J.). The candidate's framing that the deferment was "issued under the Defense Service Law" is essentially right but worth stating precisely: the deferment was an administrative/ministerial arrangement exercised under the Defense Service Law's deferment power — the case's later significance (in Rubinstein 1998) was precisely that such an open-ended arrangement of this magnitude should rest on primary legislation, not ministerial discretion. The candidate's distinctive "significant portion / judicial ceiling" claim is accurate and is genuinely the Ressler holding (not merely a later gloss): in Ressler, Barak J. warned that "there is a limit, which no reasonable Minister of Defense is authorized to exceed. Quantity becomes quality" (PD 441 at 505), and that if deferred students grew to comprise a significant portion of conscription candidates so as to harm security, the deferment would become unreasonable and must be struck down. This warning was the doctrinal hook the Court relied on a decade later in Rubinstein v. Minister of Defense (HCJ 3267/97 & 715/98, 9 Dec 1998).

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 910/86, deferment within Minister's authority, warning that growth would make it unreasonable and require striking down; justiciability/standing reversal
Docket HCJ 910/86, 1988, citation 42(2) PD 441

cases.blurb_he: העתירה נדחתה: בית המשפט קבע שהסדר דחיית השירות לבחורי ישיבה ("תורתו אומנותו"), שניתן מכוח שיקול דעתו המנהלי של שר הביטחון לפי חוק שירות ביטחון, מצוי בגדר סמכותו — ולא פסל דבר. אך שלושה שופטים בלתי-נבחרים שמרו לעצמם תקרה שיפוטית מעל הסדר שהרשויות הנבחרות הותירו פתוח: ברק קבע ש"הכמות הופכת לאיכות", וכשמספר הדחויים יגיע לחלק ניכר מבני הגיוס ההסדר ייהפך לבלתי-סביר ויחויב בביטול — והפך פררוגטיבה של הנבחרים לכוח שהערכאה רשאית להפעיל לפי שעון שקבעה היא.
cases.blurb_en: Petition denied: the Court held that the yeshiva-student service-deferment arrangement ("Torato Umanuto"), exercised under the Defense Minister's administrative discretion per the Defense Service Law, lay within his authority — striking nothing down. Yet three unelected justices reserved a judicial ceiling over an arrangement the elected branches left open-ended: Barak warned that "quantity becomes quality," and once the deferred grow to a significant portion of conscription candidates the arrangement turns unreasonable and must be struck down — converting an elected-branch prerogative into a power the bench may void on a clock of its own setting.

cases.name: בג"ץ 1715/97 לשכת מנהלי ההשקעות בישראל נ' שר האוצר (Investment Managers Bureau in Israel v. Minister of Finance)
cases.statute: חוק הסדרת העיסוק בייעוץ השקעות ובניהול תיקי השקעות, התשנ"ה-1995, סעיף 48 (Regulation of Investment Advice and Portfolio Management Law 5755-1995, sec. 48 — transitional licensing provisions)
cases.doctrine: Proportionality under the limitation clause of Basic Law: Freedom of Occupation (sec. 4). Landmark — first time the Court struck a Knesset statute under the post-1992 'constitutional revolution.'
cases.effect_reduction: The Knesset's new market-regulation regime required veteran portfolio managers/advisers already practicing before the law to sit licensing exams to keep working. The Court (24.9.1997) struck sec. 48's transitional arrangement as a disproportionate violation of freedom of occupation — the harm to experienced incumbents exceeded what was necessary — forcing the Knesset to soften the grandfathering terms and blunting the statute's intended uniform licensing barrier. (The Court upheld other challenged provisions, e.g. the bar on managing family members' portfolios.)
cases.corrected_facts: Minor refinements only (none affect publishability): (1) Decision date 24.9.1997 confirmed (ruling.co.il index also shows 27-09-1997 as a docket-index date, but the judgment date is 24.9.1997). (2) The struck provision is the transitional arrangement requiring veteran practitioners to pass licensing exams; voided as disproportionate, Knesset required to amend — exactly as claimed. (3) Prohibition on managing family members' portfolios upheld as proportionate (conflict-of-interest rationale) — confirmed. (4) Context worth adding: this is widely cited as the FIRST time the Israeli Supreme Court struck down a Knesset statute (post constitutional revolution) — a landmark fact the candidate omits. (5) The "sec. 48" label aligns with the statute's transitional provisions referenced in sources.

sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 1715/97; decided 24.9.1997; sec. 48 of the 1995 Regulation of Investment Advice and Portfolio Management Law struck as disproportionate under Basic Law: Freedom of Occupation; first statute struck since the constitutional revolution.
Lists the case among struck laws; transitional licensing provision annulled two years after entry into force for disproportionate harm to freedom of occupation.

cases.blurb_he: בפעם הראשונה מאז המהפכה החוקתית, שופטים בלתי-נבחרים פסלו דבר חקיקה של הכנסת: סעיף 48 לחוק הסדרת העיסוק בייעוץ ובניהול תיקי השקעות התשנ"ה-1995, שחייב יועצים ומנהלי תיקים ותיקים לעבור בחינות רישוי, בוטל בחלקו כפגיעה בלתי-מידתית בחופש העיסוק — ובכך נכפה על המחוקק לרכך את הסדר המעבר ולקהות את חסם הרישוי האחיד שביקש. (הוראות אחרות, כאיסור על ניהול תיקי בני-משפחה, אושרו.)
cases.blurb_en: The first time since the "constitutional revolution" that unelected judges struck a Knesset statute: sec. 48 of the 1995 Investment Advice and Portfolio Management Law — which forced veteran advisers and managers to sit licensing exams to keep practicing — was partially voided as a disproportionate violation of freedom of occupation, compelling the legislature to soften its transitional regime and blunting the uniform licensing barrier it had enacted. (Other provisions, like the bar on managing family portfolios, were upheld.)

cases.name: Tzemach v. Minister of Defense
cases.statute: Military Justice (Adjudication) Law, 5715-1955 — §237A(a), authorizing detention of a soldier for up to 96 hours before being brought before a military judge.
cases.doctrine: Proportionality (least-restrictive-means) under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty; suspended declaration of invalidity (delayed voidness) to allow corrective legislation.
cases.effect_reduction: The Court declared §237A(a) void as a disproportionate violation of personal liberty under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty, but SUSPENDED the declaration of invalidity for 12 months to give the Knesset time to legislate a constitutional alternative. The Knesset then amended the law to cut the maximum pre-judicial detention from 96 to 48 hours — so the Court's intervention forced the legislature to halve the detention power it had enacted. A landmark early strike-down with a suspended-voidness remedy.
cases.corrected_facts: No substantive corrections needed; all load-bearing facts verified. Minor naming note: the candidate calls the statute "Military Justice (Adjudication) Law, 5715-1955." Versa's authoritative English translation renders it "Military Adjudication Law, 1955" (Hebrew: חוק השיפוט הצבאי, התשט"ו-1955). These are variant translations of the same statute, not an error. The challenged/voided provision is §237A(a). Decided October 14, 1999, by an 11-justice panel; Justice Yitzhak Zamir wrote for a 10-justice majority, Justice Yaakov Kedmi dissenting. The 96→48 hour reduction is confirmed as the legislative sequel: the amendment shortening military-police pre-judicial detention from 96 to 48 hours was passed by the Knesset on March 28, 2000.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 6055/95 (consolidated 7083/95); decided Oct 14, 1999; declared §237A(a) of the Military Adjudication Law 1955 void for violating Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty; declaration of invalidity suspended 12 months; proportionality doctrine.
Lists Tzemach (HCJ 6055/95) among the statutes/provisions struck down by the Supreme Court (96-hour military detention provision invalidated; subsequently amended to 48 hours).

cases.blurb_he: סעיף 237א(א) לחוק השיפוט הצבאי, התשט"ו-1955, שהקנה מעצר חייל עד 96 שעות בטרם יובא בפני שופט, נפסל על ידי בית המשפט כפגיעה לא־מידתית בחירות שנקבעה בחוק היסוד — אך הבטלות הושעתה ל-12 חודשים, וכך כפו אחד-עשר שופטים בלתי-נבחרים על הכנסת לחתוך את סמכות המעצר שחוקקה בחצי, מ-96 ל-48 שעות.
cases.blurb_en: Section 237A(a) of the Military Adjudication Law, 1955 — which let the army detain a soldier up to 96 hours before a judge — was struck down as a disproportionate breach of liberty under the Basic Law, with invalidity suspended 12 months. Eleven unelected justices thus forced the Knesset to halve the detention power it had legislated, from 96 hours to 48.

cases.name: בג"ץ 6698/95 (HCJ 6698/95) Ka'adan v. Israel Land Administration (Katzir)
cases.statute: No Knesset statute struck; challenge to State/Israel Land Administration policy of allocating state land to the Jewish Agency for exclusively-Jewish community settlements (Katzir). Constrains the operative reach of land-allocation practice rather than a statute.
cases.doctrine: Equality as a foundational constitutional principle; the State may not discriminate on grounds of religion/nationality in allocating state land, including indirectly via the Jewish Agency
cases.effect_reduction: Landmark but NOT a statute-narrowing case — included for completeness and flagged accordingly. The Court (Barak) did not strike or read down a Knesset law; it held that the long-standing administrative/institutional practice of channeling state land through the Jewish Agency to build Jews-only community settlements violated the principle of equality, and ordered the ILA to consider the Arab petitioners' application to live in Katzir. The 'evisceration' angle is against an established settlement-allocation policy (and the implicit national-institutions framework), not against legislation. Practically, the operative effect was modest/slow (enforcement was contested for years), but doctrinally it set the equality baseline that the 2021 Nation-State ruling later leaned on. Best treated as foundational precedent rather than an interpretive-evisceration of a statute.
cases.corrected_facts: No corrections needed to the substance. Minor precision points: (1) Decided March 8, 2000; reported at 54(1) PD 258. (2) Panel: President A. Barak (majority opinion), with Justices T. Or, M. Cheshin, Y. Kedmi, and I. Zamir. (3) The operative holding was that the State may not, by law, allocate state land to the Jewish Agency for establishing the communal settlement of Katzir on a basis that discriminates between Jews and non-Jews; the Court made the order absolute and required the State to reconsider the petitioners' request on the basis of equality. The candidate's framing — landmark equality precedent, NOT a statute-narrowing case, foundational rather than an interpretive evisceration of a statute, with slow/contested practical enforcement — is accurate and is itself flagged for completeness.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: HCJ 6698/95, decided 2000; allocation of state land to Jewish Agency for Jews-only Katzir settlement held to violate equality; ILA ordered to consider Arab petitioners' application
H.C. 6698/95, 54(1) P.D. 258; State may not discriminate by nationality in allocating state land, including indirectly through the Jewish Agency

cases.blurb_he: כאן לא חוק של הכנסת נפסל אלא מדיניות מנהלית: בית המשפט בראשות ברק (2000) קבע ששיוך קרקעות מדינה לסוכנות היהודית להקמת היישוב הקהילתי קציר על בסיס מפלה בין יהודים ללא-יהודים נוגד את עקרון השוויון, וחייב את מינהל מקרקעי ישראל לשקול מחדש את בקשת העותרים הערבים. הלכת יסוד שאכיפתה בפועל נותרה איטית ושנויה במחלוקת שנים.
cases.blurb_en: No Knesset statute fell here — an administrative practice did: in 2000 the Court under Barak held that channeling state land to the Jewish Agency to build the communal settlement of Katzir on a Jews-only basis violates equality, and ordered the Israel Land Administration to reconsider the Arab petitioners' application. A foundational ruling whose real-world enforcement stayed slow and contested for years.

cases.docket: HCJ 244/00 (בג"ץ 244/00), consolidated with five further petitions
cases.name: HCJ 244/00 Mizrahi Democratic Rainbow ("Keshet") / Siyach Chadash Association for Democratic Discourse v. Minister of National Infrastructure
cases.panel: Panel of seven justices: President Aharon Barak (presiding); Justices Theodor Or, Eliyahu Matza, Tova Strasberg-Cohen, Dalia Dorner, Dorit Beinisch, and Ayala Procaccia. Lead opinion authored by Justice Theodor Or.
cases.statute: Israel Land Council Decisions (החלטות מועצת מקרקעי ישראל) No. 717, 727 and 737 — secondary land-allocation policy issued under the Israel Lands Administration Law / Basic Law: Israel Lands. Decision 727 gave kibbutzim/moshavim leasing agricultural state land 27–29% of state revenue when the land was rezoned for residential use; Decision 737 let settlements rezone and re-allocate land to members/recommendees without tender at reduced lease fees (22–66% of value); Decision 717 allowed discounted lease rates (16–51% of value) for industrial rezoning.
cases.doctrine: Equality + distributive justice in allocation of a scarce public resource (state land); reasonableness; the state as trustee of public land. Landmark on judicial review of land-policy secondary legislation.
cases.effect_reduction: The Court voided all three Council decisions ('הההחלטות בטלות ומבוטלות'), cancelling the windfall mechanism that would have transferred a large share of the value of rezoned state agricultural land to the agricultural-settlement sector. It held the distributive scheme breached equality and proper use of a public resource, forcing the Land Council/Knesset to design a new framework and ending the sector's claim to compensation/cheap re-allocation when agricultural land was converted to lucrative residential/industrial use.
cases.corrected_facts: Minor corrections/clarifications, none affecting publishability: (1) The candidate's quoted Hebrew holding "הההחלטות בטלות ומבוטלות" is a slightly garbled transcription; the actual operative phrase is "כל ההחלטות הנידונות בטלות ומבוטלות" (all the discussed decisions are null and void). (2) The petition was FILED in 2000 (hence docket 244/00); the JUDGMENT was delivered 29 August 2002. Both the candidate's year=2002 (decision) and the 244/00 docket (filing) are internally consistent. (3) The lead opinion was written by Justice Theodor Or (אור), with President Barak presiding — the candidate did not misattribute authorship, but for the record the author is Or, not Barak. (4) The principal legal ground emphasized in the sources is "extreme unreasonableness" (חוסר סבירות קיצוני) together with violation of equality and distributive justice in the allocation of a public resource — fully consistent with the candidate's characterization ("breached equality and proper use of a public resource").

sources.confirms: Docket, parties, 29 Aug 2002 date, 7-judge panel, that Decisions 717/727/737 were declared void and what each decision granted
HCJ 244/00, 'New Discourse'/Keshet petition, 28 Aug 2002 ruling against the Israel Lands Administration decisions 717/727/737 as unreasonable land-privatization windfall

cases.blurb_he: חוק רשות מקרקעי ישראל / חוק יסוד: מקרקעי ישראל: שבעה שופטים בלתי-נבחרים הורו ש"כל ההחלטות הנידונות בטלות ומבוטלות" — וביטלו את החלטות מועצת מקרקעי ישראל 717, 727 ו-737, מנגנון שהיה מעביר נתח עצום מערכּם של מקרקעי מדינה חקלאיים ששונה ייעודם למגזר ההתיישבותי. ביהמ"ש קבע שחלוקה זו לוקה בחוסר סבירות קיצוני ובפגיעה בשוויון ובאמון הציבור במשאב ציבורי, וכפה על המועצה והכנסת לעצב מסגרת חדשה.
cases.blurb_en: Israel Lands Administration Law / Basic Law: Israel Lands: a panel of seven unelected justices declared "all the discussed decisions null and void," striking Israel Land Council Decisions 717, 727 and 737 — the windfall mechanism that would have funneled a vast share of the value of rezoned state agricultural land to the settlement sector. The Court branded the scheme extremely unreasonable and a breach of equality and the state's trusteeship over a public resource, forcing the Land Council and Knesset to redraw the framework from scratch.

cases.name: Marab v. IDF Commander in the West Bank
cases.statute: NOTE: Not a Knesset statute. The instruments struck down were IDF military orders — the Order regarding Arrests During Times of War (Provisional Order) (West Bank), Order No. 1500 and its amendments 1505/1512/1518 — issued under the area commander's emergency/belligerent-occupation powers.
cases.doctrine: Belligerent-occupation law / Fourth Geneva Convention humanitarian provisions; proportionality; right to judicial review of detention; right to counsel; reading-down of permissible detention periods.
cases.effect_reduction: The military's wartime mass-arrest regime allowed holding detainees up to 18 days (later 12) before any judicial review, and up to 8 days (later reduced to 4) before interrogation/the detainee could put his case, with sweeping restrictions on lawyer access. The Court did not abolish the wartime detention authority, but declared the long no-judicial-review periods null and void as disproportionate (suspending the declaration ~6 months to let the army reorganize) and invalidated the long pre-interrogation delay, forcing far earlier judicial intervention and questioning — while upholding the lawyer-access restrictions as proportionate. The operative reach of the commander's orders was thereby cut down. Included as a landmark security-detention intervention, with the explicit caveat that the gutted instruments are military orders, not Knesset legislation, so it is a weaker fit for the 'Knesset statute' framing.
cases.corrected_facts: No material corrections needed; the characterization is accurate. Minor precision points: (1) The judgment date is 5 February 2003 (the "year=2003" is correct); official reporter citation IsrSC 57(2) 349. (2) The Court was President A. Barak with Justices Dorner and Englard. (3) On lawyer access, the Court did not merely "uphold restrictions" abstractly — it found no flaw in the framework that could permit postponing counsel access on significant security grounds for substantial periods (sources note up to ~34 days in some cases), consistent with the candidate's "upheld as proportionate" framing. (4) The candidate's caveat is correct and important: the gutted instruments are IDF military orders (Order regarding Arrests During Times of War (Provisional Order)(West Bank), Order No. 1500 and amendments 1505/1512/1518) issued under the area commander's belligerent-occupation/emergency powers — NOT a Knesset statute — so this is a weaker fit for any "Knesset statute" framing and should carry that explicit caveat.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: HCJ 3239/02, decided 5 Feb 2003; military Order 1500 and amendments; Court declared the 18/12-day pre-judicial-review periods null and void (enforcement suspended 6 months) and invalidated the long pre-interrogation delay, while upholding lawyer-access restrictions; instruments are IDF military orders, not Knesset statutes.
