sources.confirms: HCJ 3239/02 Marab et al. v. IDF Commander in the West Bank; challenge to the Order regarding Arrests During Times of War (Provisional Order); detention periods before judicial approval shortened.

cases.blurb_he: לא חוק של הכנסת — אלא צווי צבא (צו 1500 ותיקוניו), שהוצאו בידי המפקד הצבאי מכוח סמכויות החירום של הכיבוש. בית המשפט לא ביטל את סמכות המעצר בעת לחימה, אך פסל כבטל את תקופות המעצר הארוכות ללא ביקורת שיפוטית (18/12 יום) ואת העיכוב הממושך לפני חקירה כבלתי מידתיים — תוך השעיית ההכרזה כחצי שנה — וצמצם את תוקפם המעשי של הצווים, אף שאישר את הגבלות הגישה לעורך דין.
cases.blurb_en: Not a Knesset statute — but IDF military orders (Order No. 1500 and its amendments), issued by the area commander under occupation emergency powers. The Court did not abolish the wartime detention authority, yet declared void as disproportionate the long detention periods without judicial review (18/12 days) and the prolonged pre-interrogation delay — suspending the ruling ~6 months — cutting down the orders' operative reach, while upholding the restrictions on lawyer access.

cases.name: HCJ 1437/02 Association for Civil Rights in Israel (ACRI) v. Minister of Public Security
cases.date: 2004 (official reporter IsrSC 58(2) 746; precise day unconfirmed — a search surfaced "10 February 2004" but not grounded in a primary source)
cases.panel: Justice Esther Hayut (majority) and Justice Eliezer Rivlin (majority); Justice Miriam Naor (dissent)
cases.statute: Regulation 29(b) of the Prison Service Regulations, 5738-1978 (תקנות בתי הסוהר) — empowering the prison authority to prevent/restrict a prisoner's meeting with an attorney — enacted under the Prisons Ordinance
cases.doctrine: Primary arrangements + administrative legality and proportionality; a fundamental right (a detainee's/prisoner's right to consult counsel) may be restricted only by primary legislation with explicit authorization, not by secondary legislation; the enabling provision of the Prisons Ordinance did not authorize so broad a regulation
cases.effect_reduction: The Court declared Regulation 29(b) void for want of authority and for disproportionately infringing the right to counsel, but suspended the declaration (~nine months) to let the Knesset legislate — removing the executive's regulatory power to bar prisoner-lawyer meetings and forcing the matter into primary legislation
cases.corrected_facts: Party name: respondent is more commonly translated "Minister of Public Security" (השר לביטחון פנים); the candidate's "Minister of Internal Security" is the same ministry and acceptable. Official citation is IsrSC (פ"ד) 58(2) 746 (2004), which fixes the year as 2004 but not the exact day. The candidate's claim that the precise relief was a void-for-want-of-authority + disproportionality declaration suspended ~nine months for Knesset legislation is corroborated by Hebrew Wikipedia and the ACRI primary document. The exact ruling date could not be pinned down to a specific day from an authoritative primary source.

sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 1437/02, ACRI v. Minister of Internal Security, striking Prison Service Regulation 29(b) restricting prisoner-attorney consultation as requiring primary legislation and as disproportionate; suspended remedy

sources.publisher: Association for Civil Rights in Israel (petition document)
sources.confirms: HCJ 1437/02 petition by ACRI challenging the prisoner-counsel regulation

cases.blurb_he: תקנה 29(ב) לתקנות בתי הסוהר, התשל"ח-1978 — שהסמיכה את שירות בתי הסוהר למנוע או להגביל פגישת אסיר עם עורך דינו — נפסלה: בית המשפט הכריז עליה כבטלה בהיעדר הסמכה בפקודת בתי הסוהר ובשל פגיעה בלתי מידתית בזכות להיוועצות בעורך דין, אך השעה את הבטלות בכתשעה חודשים כדי לאפשר לכנסת לחוקק חוק. כך מחקו שופטים שלא נבחרו הסדר משני ששירות הכליאה הפעיל, וכפו על הרשות המבצעת והמחוקקת להעביר את העניין לחקיקה ראשית.
cases.blurb_en: Regulation 29(b) of the Prison Service Regulations, 5738-1978 — empowering the prison authority to bar or restrict a prisoner's meeting with his lawyer — was struck down: the Court declared it void for want of authority under the Prisons Ordinance and as a disproportionate infringement of the right to counsel, yet suspended the voidness for roughly nine months to let the Knesset legislate. Unelected justices erased a secondary arrangement the prison service had wielded and forced the executive and legislature to move the matter into primary law.

cases.name: Gaza Coast Regional Council v. The Knesset
cases.panel: 11-justice expanded panel headed by President Aharon Barak; petition on the core scheme rejected 10-1 (compensation provisions modified/partly invalidated)
cases.statute: Implementation of the Disengagement Plan Law, 2005 — compensation provisions
cases.doctrine: Property / dignity under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty; proportionality of compensation scheme
cases.effect_reduction: While upholding the disengagement/evacuation scheme overall, the Court invalidated several compensation provisions as disproportionate violations of property and dignity: notably it rescinded a clause barring compensation recipients from filing ordinary tort suits, and adjusted/struck restrictive elements of the compensation arrangement (e.g., the regime conditioning/limiting evacuees' choices). The Knesset's compensation design was partially voided and had to be made more generous.
cases.corrected_facts: Docket, parties, statute, year, and partial-strike action all verified. Refinements to the candidate's wording: (1) The panel was 11 justices headed by Aharon Barak; the result on the core evacuation scheme was 10-1. (2) The Court upheld the disengagement/evacuation framework but held the COMPENSATION arrangement failed the proportionality requirement and modified/struck several elements — specifically the age-21 threshold for individual grants (extended to those under 21), the residence-duration calculation, and restrictions that channeled/limited how evacuees could pursue compensation (the Court loosened these so evacuees were not confined to the statutory track alone). (3) CAUTION on the candidate's specific 'barred ordinary tort suits' phrasing: the well-known unanimous invalidation of a statutory bar on TORT suits is a DIFFERENT case — HCJ 8276/05 Adalah v. Minister of Defense (Dec 12, 2006), striking an amendment to the Civil Wrongs (Liability of the State) Law affecting Palestinians, NOT the disengagement evacuees. The disengagement ruling did open additional compensation routes for evacuees, so the general thrust (loosening restrictive limits on compensation) is correct, but the exact 'tort suit bar' language should not be lifted verbatim, to avoid conflating the two cases. Citation form: IsrSC 59(2) 481 (2005). Date 09.06.2005.

sources.confirms: HCJ 1661/05; June 9 2005; Court rescinded four financial/compensation arrangements including the bar on standard damages suits while upholding the pullout
Gaza Coast (Hof Aza) Regional Council disengagement litigation context

cases.blurb_he: חוק יישום תכנית ההתנתקות, התשס"ה-2005: הרכב של 11 שופטים בראשות אהרן ברק אישר את מתווה הפינוי עצמו (10 מול 1), אך פסל חלקית את הסדר הפיצויים כפגיעה לא-מידתית בקניין ובכבוד — הרחיב את מענק הגיל מתחת לגיל 21, תיקן את חישוב תקופת המגורים, וריכך הגבלות שתיעלו את המפונים למסלול הפיצוי הסטטוטורי בלבד. בית משפט לא-נבחר חייב את הכנסת הנבחרת להעניק פיצוי נדיב יותר מזה שעיצבה בחוק.
cases.blurb_en: Implementation of the Disengagement Plan Law, 2005: an 11-justice panel under Aharon Barak upheld the evacuation scheme itself (10-1) but partially struck the compensation arrangement as a disproportionate violation of property and dignity — extending the individual grant to those under 21, fixing the residence-duration calculation, and loosening restrictions that confined evacuees to the statutory compensation track alone. An unelected court forced the elected Knesset to pay more generously than the law it had enacted provided.

cases.name: Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. The Knesset (2006 Tal Law monitoring ruling)
cases.statute: Deferment of Military Service for Yeshiva Students Whose Vocation Is Torah Study Law, 5762-2002 ('Tal Law')
cases.doctrine: Equality derived from human dignity (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty); proportionality; deference pending empirical evidence
cases.effect_reduction: A nine-justice panel declined to invalidate the Tal Law despite finding it raised serious equality/human-dignity concerns, granting the State additional time to demonstrate the law could actually increase Haredi enlistment. The Court did not gut the statute here — it issued a strong constitutional warning and retained supervision, which set up the 2012 strike-down (HCJ 6298/07, already documented). Included only as context for the line; it is NOT an interpretive evisceration and NOT a strike-down.
cases.corrected_facts: Docket, year, and characterization are accurate. Full citation: HCJ 6427/02, 61(1) PD 619, decided 11 May 2006. SOURCING CORRECTION: the provided Versa URL (versa.cardozo.yu.edu/opinions/movement-quality-government-israel-v-knesset) does NOT point to this 2006 Tal Law case — it resolves to the entirely different 2024 ruling, HCJ 5658/23 (15-justice en banc decision striking down Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary / the reasonableness amendment). The two cases share a near-identical party name, a real disambiguation trap. The 2006 Tal Law case should NOT cite that Versa URL; use the IDI article instead (en.idi.org.il/articles/53301). Docket/citation independently confirmed by Quimbee (HCJ 6427/02, 61(1) PD 619, 2006) and a SciRP reference (decided 11/5/2006). Panel/outcome confirmed: nine-justice panel; President Barak's view, joined by seven others (8 of 9), gave the State additional time — until the end of the first five-year temporary-order period — to demonstrate the law could increase Haredi enlistment, while finding serious equality concerns. The Court did NOT strike down or read-down the statute here; that came later in 2012 (HCJ 6298/07).

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: HCJ 6427/02 Movement for Quality Government v. Knesset; majority held the Deferment (Tal) Law infringed human dignity/equality but the panel of nine declined to invalidate, granting time to monitor implementation
2006: panel of nine declined to invalidate the Tal Law despite equality concerns and granted additional time to monitor implementation

cases.blurb_he: חוק טל (חוק דחיית שירות לתלמידי ישיבות שתורתם אומנותם, התשס"ב-2002) עמד למבחן — והפעם בית המשפט בחר לא לפעול: הרכב של תשעה שופטים הכיר בכך שהחוק פוגע בשוויון ובכבוד האדם, אך נמנע מלבטלו ונתן למדינה ארכה להוכיח שהוא מגדיל את גיוס החרדים. עוצמה שיפוטית שדווקא בריסונה השאירה הסדר פטור־בפועל על כנו, עד הביטול ב-2012.
cases.blurb_en: The Tal Law (Deferment of Military Service for Yeshiva Students, 5762-2002) faced review — and this time the Court chose not to act: a nine-justice panel acknowledged the statute harmed equality and human dignity, yet declined to strike it, granting the State more time to prove it would raise Haredi enlistment. Judicial power that, in its very restraint, left a de facto blanket-exemption regime intact until the 2012 strike-down.

cases.name: Kav LaOved Worker's Hotline v. Government of Israel
cases.panel: President A. Barak, Vice-President Emeritus M. Cheshin, Justice E.E. Levy (opinion by Levy)
cases.statute: Entry into Israel Law, 5712-1952, section 6 — government 'binding/restrictive employment arrangement' (כבילה) tying a migrant worker's residence/work permit to a single named employer
cases.doctrine: Human dignity and liberty; proportionality; review of executive/secondary arrangements; right to terminate employment
cases.effect_reduction: The government's standing policy stamped a single employer in a migrant worker's permit; leaving that employer rendered the worker an illegal alien subject to arrest and deportation — described as a form of modern servitude. The Court declared the binding arrangement void as a disproportionate violation of human dignity and liberty and the right to quit, ordering the executive to replace it with a fairer permit regime. This struck down secondary/administrative arrangements built under the Entry into Israel Law against the executive's labor-import design.
cases.corrected_facts: No corrections needed. All candidate facts confirmed against the primary text (Israel Law Reports [2006] (1) IsrLR 260). Minor sharpening: the consolidated caption lists respondents 1-5 (Government of Israel; Minister of the Interior; Minister of Labour and Social Affairs; Association of Contractors and Builders in Israel; Association of Flower Growers Agricultural Cooperative Society Ltd). The Court relied on Entry into Israel Law 5712-1952 ss. 1, 2, 6, 6(1), 6(2), 15(a) and the Entry into Israel Regulations 5734-1974, applying the proportionality test under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty. The "modern-day slavery / employer's vassal" language is from Vice-President Cheshin.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: HCJ 4542/02, 30 March 2006, binding employment arrangement under Entry into Israel Law s.6 struck down as disproportionate violation of Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty
Full judgment text, panel, voiding of the binding arrangement

cases.blurb_he: בעתירת קו לעובד (2006) פסל בג"ץ את "הסדר הכבילה" שקבעה הממשלה מכוח סעיף 6 לחוק הכניסה לישראל, התשי"ב-1952 — אשר כבל מהגר עבודה למעסיק יחיד הנקוב ברישיונו, כך שעזיבתו הפכה אותו לשוהה בלתי-חוקי הצפוי למעצר וגירוש. שלושה שופטים בלתי-נבחרים הכריזו על ההסדר כבטל, כפגיעה בלתי-מידתית בכבוד האדם ובחירותו וב"זכות להתפטר", וכפו על הרשות לעצב מחדש את משטר ההיתרים — מה שצ'שן כינה צורה של עבדות מודרנית.
cases.blurb_en: In Kav LaOved (2006) the Court struck down the government's "binding arrangement" issued under section 6 of the Entry into Israel Law, 5712-1952 — which chained a migrant worker to one named employer, so that leaving turned the worker into an illegal alien facing arrest and deportation. Three unelected justices declared the arrangement void as a disproportionate violation of human dignity, liberty and the right to quit, forcing the executive to rebuild its permit regime — what Vice-President Cheshin called a form of modern servitude.

cases.name: בג"ץ 8300/02 גדבאן נסר נ' ממשלת ישראל (Gadban Nasser et al. v. Government of Israel)
cases.statute: פקודת מס הכנסה [נוסח חדש], סעיף 11ב — Income Tax Ordinance [New Version] 5721-1961, sec. 11B (income-tax reduction benefits awarded by an explicit named-community list, the 'national priority area' tax-benefit mechanism)
cases.doctrine: Right to equality as a derivative of human dignity (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty); 'suspect classification' / unconstitutional discrimination; suspended declaration of voidness (בטלות מושהית).
cases.effect_reduction: The Knesset had set income-tax discounts for residents of a fixed list of communities named explicitly in sec. 11B, with no objective criteria. The Court (President emerita Beinisch, with Rivlin and Grunis, 22.5.2012) held the list-based mechanism unconstitutionally discriminatory — it included Jewish communities while excluding adjacent Arab/Druze communities of equal or worse socioeconomic standing. It ordered Arab communities (Mi'ilya/Mazra'a, Kisra-Sumei) and the Druze Beit Jann added, removed several Jewish communities (Arad, Beit She'an, Hatzor, etc.), and gave the legislature a one-year suspension to build neutral criteria — thereby overriding the legislature's hand-picked beneficiary list. The suspension was then repeatedly extended at the State's and communities' requests, so the practical 'evisceration' of the original list played out over years.
cases.corrected_facts:
VERIFIED against the official Supreme Court judgment text (signature page: judgment 02083000_N48, "ניתן היום, א' בסיון התשע"ב (22.05.2012)") plus IDI and Ynet. Confirmed: docket בג"ץ 8300/02 (Gadban Nasser), lead of 8 consolidated petitions; panel President emerita D. Beinisch (lead opinion), President A. Grunis, Deputy President A. Rivlin; decided 22.5.2012; held the list-based income-tax-benefit mechanism unconstitutionally discriminatory for lacking clear/objective criteria, favoring some communities over equally-situated neighbors. Remedy: read benefits INTO the law for three northern Arab/Druze communities (Mazra'a, Kisra-Sumei, Beit Jann — per petitions 8300/02, 463/06, 4659/06) within one year, and declared the SUSPENDED INVALIDITY (בטלות מושהית, one year from judgment) of the inclusion of five Jewish authorities. So "partially struck" is accurate.

TWO STATUTE/FACT CORRECTIONS for the library:
1. STATUTE SECTION IS WRONG. The operative struck mechanism is NOT "section 11ב." The judgment explains §§11א and 11ב were REPEALED in 2003 by Amendment 134; the operative beneficiary list at issue was in the FIRST SCHEDULE, PART B (חלק ב' לתוספת הראשונה), added by AMENDMENT 146 (תיקון 146), 2005 (with 2007 הוראת שעה). The suspended invalidity attaches to Amendment 146's inclusion of the additional communities. Cite: Income Tax Ordinance [New Version], §11(ב) and First Schedule Part B, as amended by Amendments 134/146 — not "§11B" as the lone struck provision.
2. COMMUNITY LIST IS WRONG. The candidate names "Mi'ilya" and lumps Arad/Beit She'an/Hatzor as "removed." Correct: the THREE communities READ IN are Mazra'a (מזרעה), Kisra-Sumei (כיסרא-סמיע), Beit Jann (בית ג'אן) — Mi'ilya does NOT appear. The FIVE Jewish authorities whose inclusion was declared void-with-suspension are: Arava Tichona Regional Council, Eilot/Ayalot Regional Council, Arad, Hatzor HaGlilit, Beit She'an. They were not affirmatively "removed" by the Court — their statutory inclusion lapses after the one-year suspension unless the Knesset legislates neutral criteria.

ALSO: the consolidation note "incl. 2311/11" is unsupported — that docket does not appear in the judgment; the latest consolidated petition is 6832/07. The "year=2012" and "action=partially_struck" are correct.
cases.url: https://supremedecisions.court.gov.il/ (judgment file 02083000_N48.doc, ניתן 22.05.2012); IDI list: https://www.idi.org.il/articles/22273; full text PDF: https://www.capitax.co.il/Attachments/8300-02.pdf

sources.confirms: Lists this 2012 ruling among the laws struck by the Court; income-tax-benefit list mechanism held to violate equality unconstitutionally for lack of clear criteria.
May 22, 2012 ruling; sec. 11B of Income Tax Ordinance; discrimination against Arab communities; panel Beinisch, Rivlin, Grunis; one-year grace period to add communities/fix criteria.

Docket HCJ 8300/02 Nasser v. Government of Israel, decided 22.5.2012; holding that the list-based income-tax benefit mechanism violates equality unconstitutionally for absence of clear eligibility criteria.

cases.blurb_he: פקודת מס הכנסה — מנגנון הטבת המס לפי "רשימת יישובים" שמנתה הכנסת בעצמה (תוספת ראשונה חלק ב', תיקון 146). שלושה שופטים (ביניש, גרוניס, ריבלין, 22.5.2012) קבעו שהמנגנון מפלה ופסול חוקתית בהיעדר קריטריון אובייקטיבי: הם הוסיפו בכפייה שלושה יישובים ערביים ודרוזיים (מזרעה, כיסרא-סמיע, בית ג'אן) והכריזו על בטלות מושהית של צירוף חמש רשויות יהודיות — מוחקים את הרשימה שבחרה הנבחרת.
cases.blurb_en: Income Tax Ordinance — the income-tax-benefit mechanism built on a list of communities the Knesset itself hand-picked (First Schedule Part B, Amendment 146). Three justices (Beinisch, Grunis, Rivlin, 22 May 2012) declared the list unconstitutionally discriminatory for lacking objective criteria: they forced three Arab/Druze communities in (Mazra'a, Kisra-Sumei, Beit Jann) and suspended-voided the inclusion of five Jewish authorities — rewriting the elected legislature's chosen beneficiary list.

cases.docket: HCJ 10662/04 (joined with HCJ 3282/05 and HCJ 7804/05) — בג"ץ 10662/04 סלאח חסן נ' המוסד לביטוח לאומי; official citation PD 65(1) 782.
cases.name: HCJ 10662/04 Salah Hassan v. National Insurance Institute
cases.panel: Seven-justice panel led by President Dorit Beinisch, with Justices Miriam Naor, Edna Arbel, Elyakim Rubinstein, Salim Joubran, Esther Hayut, and Uzi Fogelman. (Notably Beinisch's final judgment before retiring.)
cases.statute: Income Support (Guaranteed Income) Law, 5741-1980 — sec. 9A(b), the conclusive presumption disqualifying any owner/user of a private vehicle from the income-guarantee benefit
cases.doctrine: Right to a minimum of dignified human existence as part of the right to human dignity (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty); disproportionate irrebuttable presumption.
cases.effect_reduction: The Court voided the categorical vehicle-ownership bar on income-support benefits, restoring eligibility the legislature's economic cost-control provision had stripped from poor claimants. First time a statute was struck for infringing a social right (minimum dignified subsistence).
cases.corrected_facts: No corrections needed to the candidate's core claims. One sourcing caveat: an intermediate fetch of the Adalah page mis-summarized the provision as "section 28," but this was a summarization error — the provision struck down is unambiguously Section 9A(b) of the Income Support (Guaranteed Income) Law, 5741-1980, as confirmed by Hebrew Wikipedia, Hamichlol, and the search synthesis of the primary PDF. The ruling was given 28 Feb 2012 with the declaration of voidness suspended ~6 months (to ~1 Sep 2012) to let the legislature/regulator respond.

sources.confirms: Docket 10662/04, decided 28 Feb 2012, struck sec. 9A(b) of the Income Support Law (vehicle disqualification), panel of 7 under Beinisch, first strike on a social-right ground
Official judgment HCJ 10662/04 Salah Hassan v. National Insurance Institute
Hassan v. NII struck Income Support Law provision denying benefit to vehicle owners as violating minimum human dignity; first social-right strike

cases.blurb_he: מותב של שבעה בראשות הנשיאה ביניש פסל את סעיף 9א(ב) לחוק הבטחת הכנסה, התשמ"א-1980 — חזקת בעלות/שימוש ברכב ששללה גורפת קצבת הבטחת הכנסה — וקבע כי שלילה קטגורית של מינימום קיום אנושי בכבוד פוגעת בכבוד האדם באופן בלתי-מידתי. בית משפט בלתי-נבחר ביטל הוראת חיסכון תקציבית שהמחוקק חוקק כדי לרסן הוצאה, והשיב לעניים זכאות שהחוק נטל מהם — הפעם הראשונה שחוק נפסל בעילה של פגיעה בזכות חברתית (ביצוע הבטלות הושהה כחצי שנה).
cases.blurb_en: A seven-justice bench led by President Beinisch struck down section 9A(b) of the Income Support (Guaranteed Income) Law, 5741-1980 — the conclusive presumption disqualifying any vehicle owner/user from the income-guarantee benefit — holding that categorically denying a minimum of dignified subsistence disproportionately violates human dignity. An unelected court voided a budgetary cost-control provision the legislature enacted to curb spending, restoring to the poor an eligibility the statute had stripped from them — the first time a law was struck on a social-right ground (the declaration of voidness suspended roughly six months).

cases.name: Tel Aviv-Jaffa District Commander v. Israel Internet Association
cases.panel: Justice Uzi Vogelman (majority opinion); President Asher Grunis (concurring); Justice Noam Sohlberg (dissenting)
cases.statute: Penal Law, 5737-1977 (esp. sec. 229 — power to close 'premises' used for gambling); the police practice of ordering ISPs to block access to online gambling sites
cases.doctrine: Primary arrangements (הסדרים ראשוניים); no power to conscript third parties (ISPs) into enforcement absent explicit statutory authority; freedom of expression
cases.effect_reduction: The Court rejected the State's appeal and affirmed that the police lacked statutory authority to order ISPs to block access to gambling websites — the closure power for physical 'premises' does not extend to website blocking, which infringes free expression and requires explicit primary legislation. The administrative blocking orders were void, stripping the police of a self-asserted regulatory power and forcing the matter to the Knesset (which enacted the 2017 Website-Blocking Powers Law).
cases.corrected_facts: Docket, parties, year (2013), statute (sec. 229 Penal Law, 5737-1977), and outcome (State's appeal REJECTED; police lacked authority to order ISPs to block gambling sites) all confirmed. Two refinements to the candidate's characterization: (1) The PRIMARY ratio per the majority (Vogelman) was NOT that "closure of premises doesn't extend to websites" — the majority actually accepted that a gambling website may be viewed as "a place" and blocking as "closing" within sec. 229(A)(1). The decisive ground was the ABSENCE of express statutory authority to compel private third parties (the ISPs) to assist in implementing the closure power. The free-expression concern is present but secondary to the third-party-authority holding. (2) The decision was NOT unanimous: Justice Sohlberg dissented, arguing the authority existed. The candidate's "stripped police of self-asserted regulatory power / forced matter to Knesset" framing is accurate in substance.

sources.confirms: Supreme Court ruling 24.3.2013 that police have no authority to order ISPs to block gambling sites; panel Grunis/Vogelman/Sohlberg; requires explicit legislation
AAA 3782/12; police lack authority; blocking requires primary legislation; ISPs not 'the long arm of the police'

cases.blurb_he: המשטרה ניכסה לעצמה סמכות לצוות על ספקיות האינטרנט לחסום אתרי הימורים, בהישען על סמכות סגירת "מקום" לפי סעיף 229 לחוק העונשין, התשל"ז-1977; בית המשפט העליון דחה את ערעור המדינה וקבע — אף שהכיר בכך שאתר עשוי להיחשב "מקום" וחסימה כ"סגירה" — שאין כל הסמכה מפורשת לגייס צד שלישי פרטי לאכיפה, ובכך פסל כסמכות שלא הייתה וגזר על המשטרה לחזור לכנסת לחקיקה ראשית. שופט אחד (סולברג) חלק וסבר שהסמכות קיימת.
cases.blurb_en: Police had arrogated to themselves a power to order ISPs to block gambling sites, stretching the authority to close a "place" under sec. 229 of the Penal Law, 5737-1977; the Supreme Court dismissed the State's appeal and held — even while accepting that a website might be a "place" and blocking a "closure" — that no express statute authorized conscripting a private third party into enforcement, voiding a power the legislature never granted and sending the police back to the Knesset for primary legislation. One justice (Sohlberg) dissented, finding the authority did exist.

cases.name: Eitan – Israeli Immigration Policy Center v. Government of Israel — HCJ 7385/13
cases.statute: Prevention of Infiltration (Offences and Jurisdiction) Law, 5714-1954, as amended by Amendment No. 4 (5774-2013) — detention/residency-center provisions (notably section 30A and the 'Holot' open-facility chapter).
cases.doctrine: Proportionality review under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty, combined with a narrowing interpretive rule that detention is permissible only where an effective removal process exists. The Court voided the one-year detention provision but, as to the residency facility, read in time and operational limits rather than only striking.
cases.effect_reduction: The Knesset's amendment authorized prolonged detention of asylum-seekers ('infiltrators') for up to a year and indefinite holding in the 'Holot' facility to pressure them to leave. The Court struck the year-long detention (section 30A) as disproportionate and held that a person cannot be detained where no effective deportation is possible — narrowing the broad detention authority the text conferred. This is a hybrid: a partial strike plus interpretive constraints capping the practical use of the residency-center regime, sharply reducing the deterrent/attrition effect the legislature intended. Included as a landmark intervention with a strong reading-down component, though the headline act was a partial strike (hence not a pure interpretive-evisceration case). NOTE: this case sits near the existing 8665/14 (Desta) line on the same statute — confirm it is not a duplicate before publishing.
cases.corrected_facts: Tighten the disposition. Per Versa (Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project), the Court did NOT primarily read down the Holot regime via interpretive constraints; it ANNULLED both pillars of Amendment No. 4 outright: (1) section 30A (one-year detention) struck as unconstitutional, including the principle that detention is impermissible where there is no practical expectation of removal; and (2) Chapter D, the entire Holot residency-center chapter, struck down for lacking any maximum duration or release criteria and for operating with detention-center characteristics (three daily reporting requirements, mandatory nighttime presence). So this is a partial strike on two fronts, not a strike-plus-read-down hybrid; the candidate's strong reading-down framing overstates the interpretive element since Holot was voided, not narrowed. The partially_struck label is otherwise correct (other Amendment 4 parts survived; the Knesset re-legislated via Amendment No. 5, reviewed separately in Desta HCJ 8665/14, 2015). Decision date: September 22, 2014. Frequently cited jointly with companion docket HCJ 8425/13.

sources.confirms: Eitan – Israeli Immigration Policy Center et al. v. Government of Israel; review of Prevention of Infiltration Law Amendment No. 4; detention provisions and residency-center regime.
Court upheld the anti-infiltration law but limited migrants' detention time; struck the one-year detention provision while constraining the facility regime.

cases.blurb_he: תיקון מס' 4 לחוק למניעת הסתננות (התשע"ד-2013) הקים מנגנון להרתעת מבקשי מקלט: מעצר סגור לשנה ושהייה בלתי-מוגבלת ב"חולות". בית המשפט (הנשיאה נאור) פסל את שני העמודים — סעיף 30א וכל פרק "חולות" — והשעה את הבטלות ל-90 יום כדי שהכנסת תפרק את ההסדר. שופטים בלתי-נבחרים ביטלו את נסיון החקיקה השני של המחוקק הנבחר.
cases.blurb_en: Amendment No. 4 to the Prevention of Infiltration Law (2013) built a deterrence machine against asylum seekers: a year of closed detention plus open-ended confinement at "Holot." The Court (President Naor) struck both pillars — section 30A and the entire Holot chapter — voiding the elected legislature's second attempt and suspending the annulment 90 days for the Knesset to dismantle it.

cases.name: Eitan – Israeli Immigration Policy Center v. The Government
cases.statute: Law for the Prevention of Infiltration (Offenses and Jurisdiction), Amendment No. 4 and Temporary Order, 5774-2013 — establishing the Holot "open" detention facility and one-year closed detention at Saharonim
cases.doctrine: Disproportionate infringement of the right to liberty and human dignity under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty (limitation clause / proportionality)
cases.effect_reduction: After Amendment No. 3's three-year detention was struck in Adam (2013, already documented), the Knesset re-legislated via Amendment No. 4 with one-year closed detention plus an effectively indefinite stay at the Holot 'open' facility. The Court struck the indefinite-Holot provision and the one-year detention clause, and ordered Holot closed within 90 days — gutting the Knesset's second attempt to detain asylum seekers and forcing the legislature into a third amendment (8665/14, round 3).
cases.corrected_facts: Decision date: 22 September 2014. The Court (majority, per President M. Naor) struck two pillars of Amendment No. 4: (a) the one-year closed-detention clause (section 30A) for new entrants, holding detention unconstitutional where there is no practical expectation of removal; and (b) the Chapter D "Holot" open-facility residence provision, which lacked a maximum-stay limit (the 20-month residence was found disproportionate). Refinement on the "ordered closed within 90 days" phrasing: the Court did not order the physical building permanently closed in 2014. It declared Chapter D's annulment but SUSPENDED that declaration for 90 days, giving the Knesset 90 days to dismantle the Holot arrangement / release residents and re-legislate. (Holot was physically shut only in March 2018, after the round-3 regime under Amendment No. 5 expired.) The "third amendment / round 3" referenced by the candidate is HCJ 8665/14 (Desta v. Knesset) — docket correct; that round challenged Amendment No. 5 (not "Amendment No. 4 round 3"). Otherwise the candidate's characterization is accurate.

sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 8425/13 / 7385/13, Amendment No. 4 / Temporary Order 5774-2013, nine-justice panel, challenge to one-year detention and indefinite Holot stay

sources.publisher: Association for Civil Rights in Israel (ACRI)
sources.confirms: Sept 22, 2014 ruling annulling Amendment No. 4 provisions and ordering Holot closed within 90 days
Full judgment text under docket HCJ 7385/13, 8425/13 striking provisions of Amendment No. 4

cases.blurb_he: חוק ועדות הקבלה (תיקון מס' 8 לפקודת האגודות השיתופיות, 2011), המתיר ליישובים קטנים בנגב ובגליל לסנן מועמדים לפי "התאמה חברתית-תרבותית" — נותר על כנו במלואו. ברוב של 5 מול 4 דחו השופטים את העתירות בטענת חוסר בשלות, בלי לפסול ובלי לצמצם; ארבעה ביקשו לבטל את החוק כפגיעה בשוויון ובכבוד. כאן בית המשפט הלא-נבחר דווקא לא נגע בחקיקת הכנסת — והשאיר מנגנון הדרה לפעול.
cases.blurb_en: The Admissions Committees Law (Cooperative Societies Ordinance Amendment No. 8, 2011), letting small Negev and Galilee communities screen applicants for "social-cultural suitability," was left wholly intact. By 5-4 the justices dismissed the petitions as unripe — neither striking nor narrowing the statute — while four would have voided it as a violation of equality and dignity. Here the unelected court chose NOT to touch the Knesset's law, leaving a gatekeeping mechanism standing.

cases.docket: HCJ 2504/11 (consolidated with HCJ 2311/11, Sabah v. Knesset)
cases.name: Sabah v. Knesset (Admissions Committees Law)
cases.statute: Admissions Committees Law — Cooperative Societies Ordinance (Amendment No. 8), 2011, allowing small communities on state land in the Negev and Galilee to vet applicants via admissions committees
cases.doctrine: Ripeness / non-justiciability ('lack of ripeness' for constitutional review); narrowing concessions noted but not adopted as binding limits
cases.effect_reduction: Weaker fit — included with caveats. By a 5-4 split the Court DISMISSED the petitions on ripeness grounds, leaving the statute fully intact (it did NOT read it down). Four justices would have struck it wholly or partially as a disproportionate violation of equality, dignity and privacy. The narrowing here is softer: the majority declined to rule because no concrete discriminatory application was yet shown, and the law on its face bars rejection on grounds of race, religion, nationality (the operative exclusion runs through vaguer 'social/cultural suitability' criteria). This is more an avoidance/deferral than an interpretive evisceration; the law's practical segregationist effect was largely preserved, which is why critics (Adalah) call it a legalization of segregation rather than a narrowing. Offered as a landmark land-allocation statute case, not as a clean evisceration.
cases.corrected_facts: No substantive corrections needed; the candidate's characterization is accurate. Minor clarifications: (1) The case caption "Sabah v. Knesset" derives from the lead petitioner in HCJ 2311/11; Adalah's own materials refer to the consolidated proceeding by both docket numbers (2504/11 and 2311/11) and label it "Adalah et al. v. The Knesset." Both dockets are real and refer to the same consolidated case. (2) Decision delivered 17 September 2014 by an expanded 9-justice panel. (3) The 5-justice majority dismissed on non-ripeness/non-justiciability grounds ("cannot determine at this stage whether the law violates constitutional rights"); 4 justices in dissent would have struck Amendment No. 8 wholly or partially as a disproportionate violation of equality, dignity, and privacy. The candidate correctly frames this as avoidance/deferral leaving the statute fully intact, NOT a read-down or interpretive narrowing.

sources.confirms: Sept 17 2014, 5-4 dismissal on ripeness; four justices found the law wholly/partially unconstitutional for violating equality, dignity, privacy; law upheld/left intact

The challenged statute is the Cooperative Societies Ordinance Admissions Committees amendments (Amendment No. 8 and 12), governing admissions committees in Negev/Galilee community settlements on state land

cases.blurb_he: חוק ועדות הקבלה (תיקון מס' 8 לפקודת האגודות השיתופיות, 2011), המתיר ליישובים קטנים בנגב ובגליל לסנן מועמדים לפי "התאמה חברתית-תרבותית". בדיון מורחב, ברוב של 5 מול 4, בית המשפט דחה את העתירות מטעמי "היעדר בשלות" — והותיר את החוק שלם וחי. ארבעה שופטים ביקשו לפסול אותו כפגיעה בשוויון ובכבוד; הרוב בחר לא להכריע. כאן הערכאה הלא-נבחרת לא צמצמה את חקיקת הכנסת — היא נמנעה מלגעת בה, והשאירה מנגנון הדרה על כנו.
cases.blurb_en: The Admissions Committees Law (Amendment No. 8 to the Cooperative Societies Ordinance, 2011) lets small Negev and Galilee communities screen applicants for "social-cultural suitability." Sitting expanded, the Court 5-4 DISMISSED the petitions as "unripe" — leaving the statute fully intact. Four justices would have struck it as a violation of equality and dignity; the majority chose not to rule. Here the unelected court did not narrow the Knesset's law — it declined to touch it, leaving a gatekeeping mechanism standing.

cases.name: Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. The Knesset (2017 conscription ruling)
cases.statute: Amendments No. 19 (2014) and No. 21 (Nov. 2015) to the Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986 — the post-Tal-Law framework for deferment/exemption of Haredi yeshiva students
cases.doctrine: Right to equality derived from human dignity (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty); proportionality / limitation clause; equal sharing of the military-service burden
cases.effect_reduction: An expanded bench (8-1, opinion by President Miriam Naor) struck down Amendments 19 and 21 as unconstitutional violations of equality and human dignity. The core objection was that Amendment 21 (passed under Haredi-party pressure) had hollowed out the enlistment framework: it pushed back the 'adjustment'/final-status periods (to 2020) and stripped out the binding conscription targets and the personal and institutional sanctions, so that — per IDI — the law 'emptied the original law of any meaning' and returned the situation to the pre-Tal-Law status quo of de facto blanket exemption. The Court treated the legislature's attempt to perpetuate the exemption-by-attrition as a circumvention of equality and voided it, while suspending the declaration of invalidity for one year (to 12 Sept 2018) to allow replacement legislation.
cases.corrected_facts: Minor precision note (not an error in substance): the bench comprised nine justices; the vote was 8-1 (eight in the majority striking down the law, Justice Noam Sohlberg dissenting). The candidate's "expanded bench (8-1)" is accurate — it was an expanded/enlarged panel of nine. Date of judgment: September 12, 2017. President Miriam Naor authored the central majority opinion. Note also: the provided IDI URL https://en.idi.org.il/articles/53301 confirms "2017 – Amendment 21 was struck down by the High Court of Justice for violating the principle of equality"; the other IDI URL (63729) in current form discusses later 2024-2025 developments rather than the 2017 ruling specifically, but is still a relevant IDI explainer on the law's trajectory.

sources.publisher: U.S. Library of Congress — Global Legal Monitor
sources.confirms: 12 Sept 2017 ruling, 8-1, struck down Amendments 19 and 21 of the Defense Service Law as violating equality/human dignity; opinion by Court President Miriam Naor
2017 Movement for Quality Government petition: Court abolished Amendments 19 and 21 due to equality violations; Amendment 21 'emptied the original law of any meaning' by removing targets/sanctions

sources.publisher: Israel Democracy Institute (IDI) — 'Rise and Fall of the (Non-)Conscription Law'
sources.confirms: Amendment 21 (Nov 2015) extended adjustment period to 2020 and emptied the conscription framework of binding targets/sanctions; Court voided it with declaration suspended one year (effective 12 Sept 2018)

cases.blurb_he: תיקונים 19 ו-21 לחוק שירות ביטחון — המסגרת שנועדה להסדיר את דחיית גיוסם של בחורי הישיבות החרדים — נפסלו (8 מול 1, בנשיאות נאור) כפגיעה בלתי-מידתית בשוויון ובכבוד האדם. אחד-עשר שופטים לא נבחרו ביטלו חקיקה של הכנסת הנבחרת; תוקף הביטול הושהה בשנה כדי לאפשר חקיקה חלופית.
cases.blurb_en: Amendments 19 and 21 to the Defense Service Law — the framework regulating deferment of Haredi yeshiva students' conscription — were struck down (8-1, President Naor) as a disproportionate violation of equality and human dignity. Unelected justices voided the elected Knesset's statute; the declaration of invalidity was suspended one year for replacement legislation.

cases.name: בג"ץ 10042/16 צחי קווטינסקי נ' כנסת ישראל (Kvatinsky v. Knesset)
cases.statute: פרק י"ב לחוק ההתייעלות הכלכלית (תיקוני חקיקה ליישום המדיניות הכלכלית לשנות התקציב 2017 ו-2018), התשע"ז-2016 — מס ריבוי דירות (Chapter 12 of the Economic Efficiency Law (Legislative Amendments for Budget Years 2017-2018) 5777-2016: the Multiple-Apartment / 'third home' tax)
cases.doctrine: Defect in the legislative process / 'principle of participation' (פגם בהליך החקיקה היורד לשורש ההליך). First Israeli case to void a statute on procedural rather than substantive grounds.
cases.effect_reduction: The government's flagship anti-housing-speculation measure — an annual tax on owners of three or more dwellings, a centerpiece of Finance Minister Kahlon's housing policy — was nullified in full (6.8.2017, five-justice panel: Naor, Hayut, Hendel, Solberg; Mazuz dissenting). The Court found the final text was dropped on the Finance Committee mere hours before the vote, denying MKs the ability to form a substantive position, a flaw 'striking at the root' of the process. The entire tax chapter was struck and taxes already collected were refunded, defeating the Knesset's enacted fiscal-regulatory scheme.
cases.corrected_facts:
Docket, parties, date (6.8.2017), statute (Chapter 12 of the Economic Efficiency Law for Budget Years 2017-2018, 5777-2016 — the multiple-apartment / "third apartment" tax מס ריבוי דירות), and panel are all confirmed: majority Naor, Hayut, Hendel, Solberg; Mazuz dissenting (5-justice panel). Landmark holding confirmed: this is the FIRST time the Supreme Court nullified a statute for a defect in the LEGISLATIVE PROCESS (violation of the "participation principle" / זכות ההשתתפות) rather than for unconstitutional content — the final text was given to MKs hours before the late-night vote, denying meaningful deliberation.

CORRECTION to characterization: The candidate's framing that the chapter was struck "in full" and that "taxes already collected were refunded, defeating the Knesset's enacted scheme" overstates the disposition. Sources (Hebrew Wikipedia, Calcalist, IDI) describe a RELATIVE/PARTIAL nullification (בטלות יחסית): the Court invalidated the tax arrangement back to the stage of the procedural defect and RETURNED it to the Knesset Finance Committee, expressly permitting the Knesset to re-enact it without repeating the entire legislative process. The Court did NOT hold the tax itself unconstitutional — on the contrary, the substantive tax was treated as constitutionally permissible; only the process was flawed. No reviewed source supports the specific "taxes already collected were refunded" assertion (Calcalist explicitly states it gives no refund information); that detail should be removed or independently sourced before publication. In practice the Knesset did not re-pass the tax, so it effectively lapsed — but that is a consequence of legislative inaction, not a court-ordered refund/full strike.

sources.confirms: Docket HCJ 10042/16; decided 6.8.2017; Chapter 12 of Economic Efficiency Law (multiple-apartment tax) struck on legislative-process grounds; first statute voided for procedural defect; five-justice panel with Mazuz dissenting.
Lists the third-apartment tax as struck by the Court due to a fundamental flaw in the legislative process.
Third-apartment tax invalidated (2017) for procedural defect: law text given to MKs minutes before debate, precluding substantive discussion.

cases.blurb_he: מס ריבוי הדירות — "מס הדירה השלישית", גולת הכותרת של מדיניות הדיור של האוצר — בוטל בידי חמישה שופטים לא-נבחרים (מזוז בדעת מיעוט) לא משום שהמס פסול, אלא בשל פגם בהליך החקיקה: לראשונה אי-פעם, פרק שלם בחוק ההתייעלות הכלכלית הוכרז בטל כי הנוסח הונח בפני חברי הכנסת שעות לפני ההצבעה. בטלות יחסית, והעניין הוחזר לוועדת הכספים לחקיקה מחדש; הכנסת לא חוקקה שוב, והמס פקע.
cases.blurb_en: The multiple-apartment "third home" tax — the centerpiece of the Finance Minister's housing policy — was nullified by five unelected justices (Mazuz dissenting) not because the tax was unconstitutional, but for a defect in the legislative process: for the first time ever, an entire chapter of the Economic Efficiency Law was voided because MKs got the final text mere hours before the vote. A relative nullification, returned to the Finance Committee to re-enact; the Knesset never did, and the tax lapsed.

cases.name: Garsegeber v. The Knesset — Deposit Law (20% wage deduction)
cases.statute: Section 4 of the Prevention of Infiltration and Ensuring Departure of Infiltrators from Israel (Legislative Amendments and Temporary Provisions) Law, 5775-2014 (the 'Deposit Law'), which amended the Foreign Workers Law, 5751-1991, requiring 20% of asylum seekers' wages be withheld in a fund payable only on departure
cases.doctrine: Disproportionate infringement of the right to property under Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty (limitation clause / proportionality)
cases.effect_reduction: The Deposit Law was designed to pressure asylum seekers to 'self-deport' by withholding a fifth of their already-meager wages, releasable only upon leaving Israel. By an 8-1 majority the Court struck the 20% employee-wage deduction (leaving only the 16% employer contribution standing), eliminating the law's coercive economic-pressure mechanism and ordering the state to return ~80 million shekels to roughly 36,000 asylum seekers within 30 days — neutralizing the Knesset's central departure-incentive tool.
cases.corrected_facts: Core claim fully verified. Minor refinements/cautions: (1) Date of judgment is 23–24 April 2020 (commonly cited April 23, 2020; Versa lists April 24, 2020) — consistent with year=2020. (2) The case caption per Versa is "Garsegeber v. Knesset"; "Gersgeber" is an alternate transliteration. (3) Statute description is accurate: Section 4 of the Prevention of Infiltration (Temporary Provisions) Law, 5775-2014 (the "Deposit Law"), which amended the Foreign Workers Law, 5751-1991, requiring a combined 36% deposit (20% deducted from the worker's wage + 16% employer contribution), payable only on departure. (4) The 8-1 majority and the action — striking ONLY the employee 20% wage-deduction component as an unconstitutional infringement of the property right, while leaving the 16% employer contribution standing, and ordering the state to return the withheld employee deposits within 30 days — are all confirmed by Versa (the authoritative Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project) and the official court verdict file (supremedecisions.court.gov.il, file 17022930). (5) NOTE/UNVERIFIED DETAIL: the specific figures "~80 million shekels" and "roughly 36,000 asylum seekers" were NOT confirmed by the authoritative sources reviewed; the refund order itself is confirmed but the precise amount and headcount should be treated as unverified and either sourced separately or softened before publication.

sources.publisher: Versa / Cardozo Israeli Supreme Court Project
sources.confirms: HCJ 2293/17 Garsegeber v. Knesset, April 24, 2020, 8-1 majority striking Section 4 of the 2014 law (amending Foreign Workers Law) on property-rights proportionality grounds; employee 20% struck, employer 16% retained
Court struck the 20% wage-deposit requirement as unconstitutional, April 23, 2020

sources.publisher: ASSAF Aid Organization for Refugees and Asylum Seekers
sources.confirms: Deposit Law = Section 4 of Prevention of Infiltration Law; 20% deducted, payable only on departure; struck down 2020

cases.blurb_he: הכנסת חוקקה את "חוק הפיקדון" (סעיף 4 לחוק למניעת הסתננות, תיקון לחוק עובדים זרים), שניכה 20% משכר מבקשי המקלט והקפיא אותו עד יציאתם מהארץ — מנוף לחץ כלכלי שנועד לדחוק אותם ל"עזיבה מרצון". בית המשפט, ברוב של 8 מול 1, פסל את ניכוי שכר העובד כפגיעה לא-מידתית בזכות הקניין, הותיר על כנו רק את חלק המעסיק (16%) והורה להשיב לעובדים את הפיקדונות — ושופטים שלא נבחרו ביטלו את הלב הכופה של חוק נבחרי הציבור.
cases.blurb_en: The Knesset enacted the "Deposit Law" (Section 4 of the Prevention of Infiltration Law, amending the Foreign Workers Law), withholding 20% of asylum seekers' wages until they left the country — economic coercion engineered to push them toward "self-deportation." By 8-1 the Court partially struck the statute, voiding the employee wage-deduction as a disproportionate infringement of property rights while leaving the 16% employer contribution standing, and ordered the withheld deposits returned — unelected judges gutting the elected legislature's coercive core.

cases.name: National Responsibility – My Home Is Israel v. Government of Israel
cases.panel: Nine justices: President E. Hayut, Deputy President H. Melcer, and Justices N. Hendel, U. Vogelman, Y. Amit, N. Sohlberg, D. Barak-Erez, M. Mazuz, A. Baron
cases.statute: Regulation 24(1) of the Special Powers for Coping with the Novel Coronavirus (Temporary Provision) regulations — restricting participation in protests to within 1,000 meters of one's residence — enacted under the Coronavirus Law (חוק סמכויות מיוחדות להתמודדות עם נגיף הקורונה החדש (הוראת שעה), 5780-2020)
cases.doctrine: Constitutional review of secondary (emergency) legislation; disproportionate infringement of freedom of expression and the right to protest; location is an inseparable part of a protest's message, especially vis-à-vis a public official's residence
cases.effect_reduction: The Court held that the 1,000-meter protest-restriction regulation failed constitutional scrutiny and was invalid for the period it was in force (Oct 1–13, 2020), and ordered that fines imposed under it be cancelled/refunded unless the state rescinded them within 90 days — nullifying the practical enforcement of the executive's protest-limiting regulation
cases.corrected_facts: Case name in the candidate translates the Hebrew אחריות לאומית as "National Responsibility," which is correct (one machine source mistranslated it as "National Insurance" — disregard that; the candidate's rendering is accurate). The panel was 9 justices led by President Hayut. Substantively, this was a partial outcome: the Coronavirus "Authorities Law" itself and most challenged provisions were upheld; only Regulation 24(1)'s 1,000-meter protest-distance restriction was declared unconstitutional and void. Because the regulation had already expired (it was in force only Oct 1–13, 2020), the Court's relief was a retroactive declaration of voidness plus cancellation/refund of fines, not a prospective injunction. A Maariv report (July 29, 2021) corroborates the practical effect: ~18,000 citizens eligible for refunds of fines paid for violating the public-space-exit restriction during Oct 1–13, 2020, refundable at post-office branches through Oct 12, 2021.

sources.confirms: 2021-04-04 ruling; regulation 24(1) banning protests beyond 1km held unconstitutional; fines from Oct 1–13 2020 cancelled/refunded unless rescinded within 90 days; panel led by President Hayut
Docket 5469/20 and consolidated petitions, parties, nine-justice panel, the 1,000-meter protest-distance regulation at issue
Official consolidated judgment in HCJ 5469/20 / 6312/20

cases.blurb_he: תשעה שופטים, בראשות הנשיאה חיות, פסלו את תקנה 24(1) לתקנות הקורונה (חוק סמכויות מיוחדות להתמודדות עם נגיף הקורונה (הוראת שעה), התש"ף-2020), שאסרה הפגנה במרחק העולה על 1,000 מטר ממקום המגורים, כפגיעה בלתי-מידתית בחופש הביטוי ובזכות למחות — בעוד החוק עצמו ורוב הוראותיו אושרו. משהתקנה כבר פקעה (תקפה רק 1–13.10.2020), בית המשפט הבלתי-נבחר הכריז על בטלותה למפרע והורה לבטל ולהשיב את הקנסות שהוטלו על כ-18,000 אזרחים — אלא אם המדינה תחזור בה תוך 90 יום — וכך ריקן מתוכן את אכיפת מגבלת ההפגנות שקבעה הרשות המבצעת.
cases.blurb_en: A nine-justice bench under President Hayut struck down Regulation 24(1) of the Coronavirus emergency regulations (enacted under the Special Powers for Coping with the Novel Coronavirus (Temporary Provision) Law, 5780-2020), which barred protesting more than 1,000 meters from one's home, as a disproportionate violation of free expression and the right to protest — while upholding the Law itself and most of its provisions. Since the regulation had already expired (in force only Oct 1-13, 2020), the unelected Court declared it void retroactively and ordered the cancellation and refund of fines levied on some 18,000 citizens unless the state rescinded them within 90 days, hollowing out enforcement of the executive's protest-limiting rule.

cases.name: HCJ 6942/19 Tzebano (Kav LaOved & ACRI) v. Minister of Interior / Knesset — foreign-worker deposit-forfeiture case
cases.panel: Expanded 7-justice panel. Majority (6): President Esther Hayut (authoring), Deputy President Uzi Vogelman, Justices Yitzhak Amit, Daphne Barak-Erez, Anat Baron, Ofer Grosskopf. Dissent (1): Justice Noam Solberg.
cases.statute: Foreign Workers Law, 5751-1991, sec. 1A(7)(2) and sec. 1A(7)(3A), and the Foreign Workers (Deposit for Foreign Workers) Regulations, 5776-2016 — the mechanism deducting/forfeiting an escalating share of the worker's mandatory deposit (severance + employer pension component) if the worker overstayed, with total forfeiture after six months' delay.
cases.doctrine: Right to property (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty); proportionality.
cases.effect_reduction: The expanded panel voided the forfeiture/graduated-deduction mechanism as a disproportionate violation of the right to property, suspending the declaration for six months to let the Knesset craft an alternative. This neutralised the financial 'departure incentive' the legislature built into the deposit scheme — workers could no longer be stripped of their own accrued severance/pension money for overstaying. The Court stressed the mechanism was unusual even in Israeli law with no comparative precedent.
cases.corrected_facts: Lead/case name on the official record is "Tzebano v. Minister of Interior" (Tzebano being a lead migrant-worker petitioner); Kav LaOved and ACRI are co-petitioners, as the candidate states. Characterization note: the Court did not annul the mechanism immediately — it declared the graduated-deduction/forfeiture mechanism a disproportionate violation of the right to property (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty) that fails the proportionality (third subtest), and SUSPENDED the declaration of voidance for six months for the Knesset/ministers to craft an alternative; absent an alternative, the mechanism is voided in full. The candidate already states this suspension correctly, so "struck down" is accurate in the Israeli suspended-declaration-of-invalidity sense. Individual petitioners were ordered to receive their withheld funds. The "no comparative precedent / unusual mechanism" point is confirmed.

sources.confirms: July 2023 ruling, expanded 6-1 panel led by Hayut/Grosskopf opinion vs Solberg dissent, struck the deposit-forfeiture law as a disproportionate property-right violation, 6-month suspension
HCJ 6942/19; challenged Foreign Workers Law ss.1A(7)(2) & 1A(7)(3A) and the 2016 Deposit Regulations; deposit-forfeiture mechanism struck as disproportionate property-right infringement

cases.blurb_he: החלטות 717, 727 ו-737 של מועצת מקרקעי ישראל העניקו לקיבוצים ולמושבים נתח עצום מערך הקרקע הציבורית בעת הסבת קרקע חקלאית לבנייה ולתעשייה — הקצאה ללא מכרז ובדמי-חכירה מוזלים. שבעה שופטים, שאיש מהם לא נבחר, הורו כי "כל ההחלטות הנידונות בטלות ומבוטלות", ביטלו במחי יד את מנגנון רווחי-ההון של מגזר ההתיישבות בנימוק של חוסר-סבירות קיצוני ופגיעה בשוויון ובנאמנות הציבורית — וכפו על המועצה לתכנן מסגרת חדשה תחת אמות-מידה שקבע בית המשפט.
cases.blurb_en: Israel Land Council Decisions 717, 727 and 737 handed kibbutzim and moshavim a vast share of the state-land value created when farmland was rezoned for housing and industry — tender-free allocation at slashed lease fees. Seven unelected justices declared "all the decisions discussed null and void," wiping out the settlement sector's windfall mechanism for extreme unreasonableness and breach of equality and the state's trusteeship of public land — forcing the Council to rebuild the framework under court-set criteria.

cases.name: Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. Minister of Defense — HCJ 6198/23 (and joined petitions)
cases.panel: Extended bench of 9 justices, unanimous; opinion authored by Acting Supreme Court President Justice Uzi Vogelman. (Full roster of all nine names not surfaced in the English-language authoritative sources reviewed.)
cases.statute: Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986 — following expiry (June 30, 2023) of the statutory deferment arrangement and lapse of interim Government Decision 682
cases.doctrine: Rule of law / legality principle (executive may not contradict statute by administrative decision); equality
cases.effect_reduction: With no statute exempting yeshiva students, the Court unanimously held the government's decision to instruct the IDF not to draft eligible Haredim was unlawful: absent a legal exemption the universal conscription duty applies, so the state 'must act to draft' yeshiva students and may not practice selective enforcement. It further held there is no legal basis to keep transferring state funds to yeshivas for draft-age students lacking exemption status — converting the legislature's and coalition's inaction into an affirmative judicial command against their intent.
cases.corrected_facts: No substantive corrections needed. Two refinements for precision: (1) The exact ruling date is June 25, 2024 (the candidate gave only year=2024). (2) The action is more precisely a declaratory holding that the government's decision/instruction not to draft eligible Haredim, and section 3 of Government Decision (Resolution) 682, lacked any legal basis — functionally striking the policy and the resolution provision and barring continued yeshiva funding for draft-eligible students absent legislation. The candidate's "struck_down" label is acceptable as a functional characterization. Panel was a 9-justice extended bench (unanimous), opinion by Acting President Vogelman.

sources.publisher: Library of Congress, Global Legal Monitor
sources.confirms: June 25 2024 HCJ accepted petitions; absent legal framework, must draft and no legal basis to subsidize institutions; Defense Service Law
Unanimous 9-0 decision, opinion by Acting President Vogelman, state must act to draft, no selective enforcement, funding transfers prohibited from April 1 2024
High Court ordered halt to yeshiva funds for draft-eligible students; government must draft Haredi men

cases.blurb_he: משפקעה הסדרת הדחייה לבחורי ישיבה ולא נותר כל פטור בחוק שירות הביטחון, התשמ"ו-1986, פסק בית המשפט פה אחד (תשעה שופטים, מפי המשנה לנשיאה פוגלמן) כי החלטת הממשלה להורות לצה"ל שלא לגייס חרדים זכאי גיוס נעדרת בסיס חוקי: בהיעדר פטור חל חוק חובת הגיוס האוניברסלי, ואין סמכות לאכיפה בררנית. בכך הפך בית המשפט בלתי-נבחר את שתיקת הקואליציה והכנסת לצו שיפוטי פוזיטיבי המחייב לפעול לגיוס ואוסר על העברת כספי מדינה לישיבות עבור תלמידים זכאי גיוס — תוצאה הנוגדת את כוונת הרשות הנבחרת.
cases.blurb_en: With the yeshiva-deferment arrangement expired and no exemption left in the Defense Service Law [Consolidated Version], 5746-1986, a unanimous nine-justice bench (opinion by Acting President Vogelman) held the government's instruction to the IDF not to draft draft-eligible Haredim legally baseless: absent any exemption the universal conscription duty applies and selective non-enforcement is unlawful. An unelected court thus converted the coalition's and Knesset's deliberate inaction into an affirmative judicial command to draft and a bar on transferring state funds to yeshivas for draft-eligible students — a result running directly against the elected branches' intent.

cases.name: HCJ 7922/19 Tzedek Financi & Ravach Naki v. State (Chief of Staff Pension "Increases")
cases.panel: President Isaac Amit (opinion author), Justice Noam Sohlberg/—; confirmed members: President Isaac Amit, Justice David Mintz, Justice Ofer Grosskopf
cases.statute: Permanent Service in the IDF (Pensions) Law [Consolidated Version], 5745-1985 — esp. the Chief of Staff's discretionary pension-'increase' power (sec. 18(b))
cases.doctrine: Primary arrangements (הסדרים ראשוניים); a blanket, near-universal benefit cannot be created under a narrow discretionary statutory power and must be set by primary legislation
cases.effect_reduction: The Court held that granting 'Chief of Staff increases' (pension top-ups) to the overwhelming majority of career servicemembers exceeded the statutory authority conferred on the Chief of Staff — a sweeping, systemic benefit of this kind is effectively a primary arrangement that must be regulated by the Knesset, not by executive discretion. The Court ruled the practice unlawful and to be cancelled (suspended to end-2025) while protecting already-retired pensioners, pushing the matter into legislation.
cases.corrected_facts: Panel confirmed as President Isaac Amit (wrote the lead opinion), Justice David Mintz, and Justice Ofer Grosskopf (three-justice bench). One sourcing nuance: TheMarker's English auto-translation rendered the governing statute as "Civil Service Law," but the actual Hebrew quoted ("חוק שירות הקבע") is the Permanent Service (Pensions) Law, matching the candidate's stated statute — no correction to the candidate is needed, just flagging the translation artifact. The annual cost of the benefit was reported at ~NIS 1.8 billion and it reportedly benefited the large majority (~98%) of retiring career personnel, consistent with the "overwhelming majority" framing in the claim.

sources.confirms: HCJ ruling 15.9.2025 that 'Chief of Staff increases' to the vast majority of career personnel exceed the authority granted to the Chief of Staff under the Permanent Service (Pensions) Law; to be cancelled, suspended to 31.12.2025; matter to be regulated by legislation
Petition 7922/19 (2019) by Tzedek Financi & Ravach Naki; Court ruled the increases contrary to law and to be cancelled 15.9.2025; government to regularize by legislation; existing retirees not harmed

cases.blurb_he: בית המשפט קבע ש"הגדלות הרמטכ"ל" — תוספות פנסיה של כ-1.8 מיליארד ש"ח בשנה ששולמו לכ-98% מאנשי הקבע — חורגות מסמכות הרמטכ"ל לפי חוק שירות הקבע בצה"ל (גמלאות), שכן הטבה גורפת וכמעט-אוניברסלית היא "הסדר ראשוני" שחייב להיקבע בחקיקת הכנסת ולא בשיקול דעת מינהלי; ביטל את ההסדר (בהשעיה עד סוף 2025, תוך הגנה על גמלאים קיימים) ודחק את הסוגיה אל הכנסת. שופטים בלתי-נבחרים פסלו הטבת-ענק רבת-שנים והכפיפו את מימושה לרצונו הטוב של המחוקק.
cases.blurb_en: The Court struck down the "Chief of Staff increases" — pension top-ups of roughly NIS 1.8 billion a year paid to some 98% of career personnel — as exceeding the Chief of Staff's authority under the Permanent Service (Pensions) Law, holding that a blanket, near-universal benefit is a "primary arrangement" the Knesset must legislate, not executive discretion; it voided the scheme (suspended to end-2025, sparing existing retirees) and pushed the matter to the legislature. Unelected justices canceled a decades-old mass entitlement and made its survival contingent on lawmakers' goodwill.
